topics.label: The variant (two groups within your tradition)
topics.prompt: “Even small differences between groups within a religion can shape how people live.” With reference to a religious tradition you have studied, briefly explain what led to the divide between two (2) variants. Analyse the differences in belief and practice, the impact of these differences, and the ongoing cooperation or conflict between the variants.

topics.label: Religious response to suffering
topics.prompt: Drawing on the beliefs, values and practices of the second religious tradition you have studied: how has the tradition’s understanding of human nature and purpose influenced its understanding of suffering and its proposed responses to suffering?

topics.label: Religious response to an ethical issue
topics.prompt: Drawing on the beliefs, values and practices of the second tradition you have studied: how might adherents respond to a contemporary ethical issue, and what conclusions can be drawn about this response and the reasons for it?

topics.label: Depth study - the place of women
topics.prompt: Outline and explain change or lack of change in your tradition’s teaching on the place of women in religion, and the impact of this. Analyse the different points of view within the tradition. Draw on your depth study and use primary and secondary sources.

topics.label: Depth study - faith and science
topics.prompt: Outline and explain change or lack of change in your tradition’s response to the challenge brought by science and scientific endeavour, and the impact of this. Analyse different points of view within the tradition. Draw on your depth study and use primary and secondary sources.

A-variant|Christianity.topic: Section A - The Variant: comparing two major Christian variants (origin of the split, belief differences, practice differences worked FROM a named practice, and conflict AND cooperation)

whatToKnow.point: Choose ONE clean variant pair and stick to it - the safest, most defensible pair is Roman Catholicism vs Eastern Orthodoxy (the 1054 East–West Schism), because the split, beliefs and practices are clearly documented and examiners reward precision.
whatToKnow.detail: The Great Schism is conventionally dated 1054, when Cardinal Humbert of Silva Candida (legate of Pope Leo IX) and Patriarch of Constantinople Michael Cerularius issued mutual excommunications in the Hagia Sophia. The split was the culmination of centuries of drift between the Latin (Western, Rome) and Greek (Eastern, Constantinople) churches. The excommunications were finally lifted in 1965 by Pope Paul VI and Patriarch Athenagoras I in the Catholic–Orthodox Joint Declaration.

whatToKnow.point: The origin of the split was doctrinal AND political/cultural, not a single event - name the two headline causes: the Filioque clause and papal primacy.
whatToKnow.detail: (1) The Filioque ('and the Son') - the West added 'who proceeds from the Father AND the Son' to the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed (originally agreed at Constantinople 381, which said the Spirit proceeds 'from the Father'). This addition long PREDATED 1054 (first appearing at the regional Council of Toledo in 589 and only adopted into the Creed at Rome itself by the early eleventh century, c.1014), so it was already a standing grievance rather than a 1054 innovation. The East rejected this as an unauthorised change to an ecumenical creed and as bad Trinitarian theology. (2) Papal primacy/supremacy - Rome claimed the Bishop of Rome (Pope) holds universal jurisdiction as successor of St Peter; the East held the Pope to be 'first among equals' (primus inter pares) within a college of five patriarchs (the Pentarchy: Rome, Constantinople, Alexandria, Antioch, Jerusalem), with no power to override a council. Background drivers: language (Latin vs Greek), the 1054 leavened/unleavened bread dispute (azymes), and political fallout from the Sack of Constantinople by Latin crusaders in 1204, which deepened the rupture.

whatToKnow.point: Key BELIEF differences beyond the split itself - be precise, do not overstate.
whatToKnow.detail: (1) Procession of the Holy Spirit: Catholics affirm Filioque (Spirit from Father and Son - the clause first appearing at Toledo 589 and adopted at Rome by the early eleventh century, c.1014); Orthodox affirm procession from the Father alone (monopatrism). (2) Authority: Catholics affirm papal infallibility when the Pope speaks ex cathedra on faith/morals (defined at the First Vatican Council, 1870); Orthodox locate infallibility in the consensus of the Church expressed through Ecumenical Councils (they accept the first seven, up to Nicaea II, 787). (3) Salvation/sin: the West developed the doctrine of Purgatory and (historically) indulgences and a more juridical view of original sin (inherited guilt, shaped by Augustine); the East speaks of theosis (deification - becoming partakers of the divine nature, 2 Peter 1:4) and ancestral sin (inheriting mortality/corruption rather than personal guilt). (4) The Immaculate Conception of Mary (defined Catholic dogma 1854) is NOT held by the Orthodox, who venerate Mary as Theotokos ('God-bearer', defined at Ephesus 431) but reject the Immaculate Conception as resting on the Western view of original sin.

whatToKnow.point: Work FROM a concrete PRACTICE - the Eucharist/Divine Liturgy is the strongest practice to anchor the essay because the differences are visible and doctrinally loaded.
whatToKnow.detail: Catholics call the central rite the Mass; the Orthodox call it the Divine Liturgy (most commonly the Liturgy of St John Chrysostom). Visible/practical differences: (1) Bread - Catholics use unleavened bread (azymes, like a wafer); Orthodox use leavened bread (the prosphora), the original azymes dispute. (2) Both affirm the Real Presence, but Catholics explain HOW using the scholastic term transubstantiation (Aquinas, defined at the Fourth Lateran Council 1215 and Trent 1551), while the Orthodox affirm the change as a genuine mystery and generally avoid the philosophical mechanism. (3) Communion in both kinds - Orthodox laity always receive both bread AND wine, given together on a spoon; Western practice for centuries gave the laity bread only (one kind), though Vatican II broadened the chalice. (4) Setting - the Orthodox liturgy is celebrated behind an iconostasis (a screen of icons), heavily chanted, with no instrumental music; the Latin Mass historically used the organ. (5) Calendar - Orthodox Easter (Pascha) usually falls on a different date because many Orthodox churches still calculate it on the Julian calendar.

whatToKnow.point: CONFLICT between the two variants - name real historical events, not vague 'tensions'.
whatToKnow.detail: The 1054 mutual excommunications; the Sack of Constantinople (1204) during the Fourth Crusade, when Latin crusaders looted the city and Hagia Sophia and installed a Latin Patriarch - remembered by the Orthodox as a defining betrayal; failed reunion councils (Second Council of Lyon 1274 and the Council of Florence 1439, where reunion was signed but rejected by the Orthodox faithful and clergy back home); and ongoing disputes over 'Uniate' / Eastern Catholic churches (Eastern-rite churches in communion with Rome), which the Orthodox view as Catholic poaching of their flock.

whatToKnow.point: COOPERATION / reconciliation - the modern ecumenical movement is essential for an A-grade evaluation.
whatToKnow.detail: The 1965 Catholic–Orthodox Joint Declaration lifted the 1054 excommunications (Pope Paul VI and Patriarch Athenagoras I); the Joint International Commission for Theological Dialogue between the Catholic Church and the Orthodox Church has met since 1980 (e.g. the Ravenna Document 2007 on conciliarity and authority); and the historic 2016 Havana meeting between Pope Francis and Patriarch Kirill of Moscow was the first meeting between a pope and a Russian Orthodox patriarch since the schism. Both share the Nicene Creed (minus the Filioque dispute), the first seven Ecumenical Councils, apostolic succession, and seven sacraments/mysteries. On recognising each other's sacraments the position is genuine but asymmetric: the Catholic Church formally recognises Orthodox sacraments and orders as valid (Vatican II, Unitatis Redintegratio), while the Orthodox generally regard Catholic sacraments as valid by 'economy', though without a single binding pan-Orthodox declaration - a real but uneven point of commonality.

whatToKnow.point: Accuracy guardrails specific to THIS pair - what NOT to say.
whatToKnow.detail: Do NOT call the Orthodox 'Protestant' or imply they broke away in protest - Orthodoxy predates Protestantism by 500 years and sees ITSELF as the original undivided Church. Do NOT say the two churches 'don't recognise each other' - since 1965 the Catholic Church recognises Orthodox sacraments and apostolic succession, and the Orthodox generally recognise Catholic sacraments by economy; they are simply not in full communion. Do NOT confuse Eastern Orthodox with Oriental Orthodox (e.g. Coptic, Armenian) - the latter split earlier, at Chalcedon 451, over Christology (miaphysitism), which is a different schism. Do NOT say the Pope and Patriarch reconciled fully in 1965 - only the excommunications were lifted; the doctrinal disagreements (Filioque, papacy) remain unresolved.

pointsOfView.label: Roman Catholic position on authority
pointsOfView.who: Roman Catholicism (First Vatican Council, 1870; Catechism of the Catholic Church)
pointsOfView.summary: The Bishop of Rome is the visible head of the universal Church and successor of St Peter, holding supreme jurisdiction; he can teach infallibly ex cathedra. The Filioque is a legitimate clarification of Trinitarian faith. Christ founded one Church governed through Peter (Matthew 16:18, 'You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church').

pointsOfView.label: Eastern Orthodox position on authority
pointsOfView.who: Eastern Orthodoxy (e.g. articulated by theologians such as Timothy/Kallistos Ware in The Orthodox Church)
pointsOfView.summary: Authority rests in the whole Church and is expressed through Ecumenical Councils, not in a single bishop. The Pope is first in honour (primus inter pares) but not in jurisdiction. The unilateral addition of the Filioque to a creed agreed by an Ecumenical Council was both theologically wrong and a breach of conciliar authority - the West changed the common faith without the East's consent.

pointsOfView.label: Ecumenical / reconciliationist view
pointsOfView.who: Modern Catholic–Orthodox dialogue (Vatican II's Unitatis Redintegratio 1964; the Joint International Commission; Ravenna Document 2007)
pointsOfView.summary: The two churches are 'sister churches' sharing valid sacraments, apostolic succession and the undivided first millennium. Reunion is a genuine goal; remaining obstacles (papal primacy, Filioque) are being worked through theologically rather than treated as permanent. The 1054 excommunications were a human rupture that has been formally lifted.

pointsOfView.label: Scholarly historical view (the split was gradual)
pointsOfView.who: Historians such as Steven Runciman (The Eastern Schism) and Henry Chadwick (East and West: The Making of a Rift in the Church)
pointsOfView.summary: 1054 was a symbolic flashpoint, not the true cause; the East and West had been drifting apart for centuries through language, politics, liturgy and the rise of separate empires. The Sack of Constantinople in 1204 did more lasting damage to relations than the 1054 excommunications themselves, hardening the schism in the popular Orthodox memory.

sources.reference: Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed (Council of Constantinople, 381) - clause on the Holy Spirit
sources.snippet: '…the Holy Spirit… who proceeds from the Father' (the original, pre-Filioque wording the East defends).
sources.useFor: Anchoring the Filioque dispute in the actual conciliar text the West later altered (at Toledo 589, adopted in Rome by c.1014).

sources.reference: Gospel of Matthew 16:18 (public-domain, KJV/RSV)
sources.snippet: 'You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church.'
sources.useFor: The verse Catholicism uses to ground papal primacy; show how the two variants interpret the SAME text differently.

sources.reference: 2 Peter 1:4 (public-domain)
sources.snippet: '…partakers of the divine nature.'
sources.useFor: Grounding the Orthodox doctrine of theosis (deification) as a belief difference from the West's more juridical soteriology.

sources.reference: Catholic–Orthodox Joint Declaration (Paul VI and Athenagoras I, 7 December 1965)
sources.snippet: The mutual excommunications of 1054 are 'removed from the memory and midst of the Church' and consigned to oblivion.
sources.useFor: Evidence of genuine cooperation/reconciliation for the A-grade evaluation.

sources.reference: Kallistos (Timothy) Ware, The Orthodox Church (Penguin)
sources.snippet: Frames the Pope as primus inter pares and explains the Orthodox objection to the unilateral Filioque addition and to papal supremacy.
sources.useFor: Authoritative articulation of the Orthodox position; paraphrase, do not quote at length.

sources.reference: Henry Chadwick, East and West: The Making of a Rift in the Church
sources.snippet: Argues the schism was a slow estrangement of language, politics and liturgy, crystallised but not caused by 1054.
sources.useFor: Supporting the 'gradual split' analysis and the significance judgement that 1204 mattered more than 1054.

sources.reference: Steven Runciman, The Eastern Schism
sources.snippet: Emphasises the Fourth Crusade's 1204 Sack of Constantinople as the decisive psychological rupture between the churches.
sources.useFor: Evaluating which event was MOST significant in cementing the divide.

A-variant|Christianity.workedThesis: While the Great Schism of 1054 is conventionally treated as the moment Roman Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy divided, the most significant cause of their separation was not a single excommunication but a centuries-long estrangement over authority and the Filioque - a divide so deep it is still visible today in the contrast between the Mass and the Divine Liturgy, yet not so absolute that the 1965 lifting of the excommunications and ongoing ecumenical dialogue cannot point both 'sister churches' back toward the undivided faith of the first millennium.
paragraphPlan: Intro: define the two variants (Roman Catholicism, Latin/West; Eastern Orthodoxy, Greek/East), name the conventional 1054 date, and state the thesis that the split was gradual and doctrinal but is now partially bridged.

Origin of the split: explain the gradual estrangement - language (Latin/Greek), the Pentarchy vs Roman claims - then the two doctrinal flashpoints (Filioque, first at Toledo 589 and adopted in Rome by c.1014, added to the 381 Creed; papal primacy vs primus inter pares), culminating in the 1054 mutual excommunications by Humbert and Cerularius.

Belief differences: procession of the Holy Spirit (Filioque vs monopatrism), authority (papal infallibility 1870 vs the seven Ecumenical Councils), and soteriology (Purgatory/juridical original sin in the West vs theosis/ancestral sin in the East), plus the Immaculate Conception (1854) which Orthodoxy rejects.

Practice difference worked FROM the Eucharist: Mass vs Divine Liturgy of St John Chrysostom - unleavened vs leavened bread, transubstantiation vs affirmed mystery, communion in both kinds on a spoon vs (historically) one kind, the iconostasis, and the divergent Pascha date. Link each practice back to a belief.
Conflict: 1054 excommunications, the 1204 Sack of Constantinople, and the failed reunion councils of Lyon (1274) and Florence (1439); note the Uniate-church grievance.

Cooperation + EVALUATION: the 1965 Joint Declaration, the Joint International Commission/Ravenna 2007, and Havana 2016 - then weigh significance: the shared first millennium, seven councils and apostolic succession (and the asymmetric sacramental recognition) mean the divide is real but not absolute; judge which cause mattered most (1204 over 1054, per Runciman/Chadwick).
Conclusion: return to the thesis - the divide is significant enough to remain unresolved doctrinally, but the two are 'sister churches' moving toward, not away from, unity.
A-variant|Christianity.exemplarParagraph: Anchoring the comparison in a single practice - the central act of worship - reveals how significant the doctrinal split between Roman Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy truly is, because the differences are not merely ceremonial but carry the weight of the beliefs that caused the schism. The Catholic Mass uses unleavened bread (azymes, a thin wafer), while the Orthodox Divine Liturgy of St John Chrysostom uses leavened bread (the prosphora) - the very 'azymes' dispute that flared in 1054 - so that even the bread on the altar encodes the eleventh-century quarrel. More significantly, both churches affirm the Real Presence of Christ in the Eucharist, yet Catholicism explains how through the scholastic doctrine of transubstantiation (formalised at the Fourth Lateran Council in 1215 and the Council of Trent in 1551), whereas Orthodoxy deliberately leaves the change an unexplained mystery; this single contrast captures the deeper Western instinct to define and the Eastern instinct to contemplate that also drives the Filioque and papal-infallibility disputes. The practical divergences run further still - Orthodox laity always receive both the bread and the wine together on a spoon, while the Latin West for centuries withheld the chalice; the Orthodox liturgy unfolds behind an iconostasis to chant alone, while the Mass admitted the organ - and because many Orthodox churches still keep the Julian calendar, the two communions usually cannot even celebrate Easter (Pascha) on the same day. Evaluated together, these practices show that the schism is genuinely significant: it has hardened into two distinct ways of being Christian that a worshipper can see and taste. Yet the significance is not absolute, for both rites are rooted in the same apostolic succession - the Catholic Church formally recognising Orthodox sacraments as valid, and the Orthodox generally regarding Catholic sacraments as valid by economy - which is precisely why reconciliation since the 1965 lifting of the excommunications has been conceivable at all.

ladderForThis.C: DESCRIBE the differences: state that Roman Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy split in 1054, and correctly describe the key belief differences (Filioque, papal primacy) and practice differences (Mass vs Divine Liturgy, unleavened vs leavened bread, the iconostasis) with accurate names and dates.
ladderForThis.B: ANALYSE the differences: explain WHY the practices differ by linking each to a belief - e.g. show that the unleavened/leavened bread and transubstantiation/mystery contrasts express the deeper Western-define vs Eastern-contemplate divide, and that papal primacy vs the Pentarchy is the structural root of the 1054 rupture.
ladderForThis.A: EVALUATE significance: judge HOW significant the divide is and which cause mattered most - argue (using Runciman/Chadwick) that 1204 cemented the schism more than 1054, weigh the genuine commonalities (shared seven councils, apostolic succession, and the asymmetric mutual sacramental recognition) against the unresolved obstacles (Filioque, papacy), and assess whether the 1965 Declaration and Havana 2016 make reunion a real prospect or a symbolic gesture.

trapsForThisQuestion: Calling Eastern Orthodoxy 'Protestant' or saying it 'broke away in protest' - Orthodoxy predates Protestantism by ~500 years and sees itself as the original undivided Church.
Confusing Eastern Orthodox with Oriental Orthodox (Coptic, Armenian, etc.) - the Orientals split earlier at Chalcedon (451) over Christology (miaphysitism), a completely different schism.

Saying the two churches 'don't recognise each other' - the Catholic Church formally recognises Orthodox sacraments and apostolic succession, and the Orthodox generally recognise Catholic sacraments by economy; they are simply not in full communion.
Treating 1054 as the sole cause - examiners reward showing it was the culmination of a gradual estrangement (language, politics, Filioque, papacy), with 1204 arguably more decisive.
Claiming the 1965 Joint Declaration 'reunited' the churches - it only lifted the mutual excommunications; the doctrinal disagreements remain unresolved.

Vague filler like 'they have different practices' - name the practice (the Eucharist/Divine Liturgy), the specific differences (leavened bread, transubstantiation, communion on a spoon, iconostasis), and tie each to a belief.

Misattributing the Filioque - it is the WEST that added 'and the Son' to the 381 Creed (first at Toledo 589, adopted in Rome by c.1014); the East kept the original 'from the Father'. Reversing this, or dating the addition to 1054, is a serious accuracy error.

Saying only Catholics venerate Mary - both do; the precise difference is the Immaculate Conception (1854 Catholic dogma), which the Orthodox reject while still honouring Mary as Theotokos (Ephesus 431).

A-variant|Judaism.topic: Differences between two major variants of Judaism: Orthodox Judaism vs Reform (Progressive) Judaism - origin of the split, belief differences, practice differences (worked from the practice of Sabbath/shabbat observance), and conflict and cooperation

whatToKnow.point: The split has a specific time, place and cause: early-19th-century Germany, born of Jewish Emancipation and the Enlightenment (Haskalah)
whatToKnow.detail: After Napoleon and the gradual emancipation of European Jews (early 1800s), Jews could leave the ghetto and become citizens. Reform began in Germany: Israel Jacobson held reformed services in Seesen (1810) and Berlin, introducing the organ, German-language prayers and a sermon. The Hamburg Temple opened in 1818. Reform rabbis met in synods at Brunswick (1844), Frankfurt (1845) and Breslau (1846) to formalise change. Orthodoxy crystallised AS A REACTION: Rabbi Moses Sofer (the Chatam Sofer, 1762–1839) of Pressburg coined the slogan adapted from Mishnah, 'chadash asur min haTorah' - 'the new is forbidden by the Torah.' So 'Orthodox' is partly a label that only made sense once Reform existed.

whatToKnow.point: The core theological dividing line is the AUTHORITY and NATURE of halakhah (Jewish law) and Torah min haShamayim (Torah from Heaven)
whatToKnow.detail: Orthodoxy holds the Torah (both Written - the Chumash - and Oral - Mishnah/Talmud) was divinely revealed to Moses at Sinai and is binding and unchanging; halakhah is God's command, codified in the Shulchan Aruch (Joseph Karo, 1565). Reform holds revelation is PROGRESSIVE and that Torah is divinely inspired but humanly written and historically conditioned; ethical/moral commandments are binding but ritual law (kashrut, much of Shabbat detail) is a matter of personal autonomy and conscience. The 1885 Pittsburgh Platform (USA) explicitly rejected binding dietary, purity and priestly laws as no longer fitting modern civilisation.

whatToKnow.point: Belief differences flow from that authority question: Messiah, Zion, chosenness, women
whatToKnow.detail: Orthodoxy awaits a personal Messiah (mashiach), restoration of the Temple and the sacrificial system, and bodily resurrection. Classical Reform reinterpreted these: a Messianic AGE (an era of justice) rather than a personal Messiah; early Reform even removed prayers for return to Zion and rebuilding the Temple, and saw Jews as a religious community with a universal ethical 'mission to the nations' rather than a nation-in-exile. On gender, Orthodoxy maintains separate roles and an all-male rabbinate and minyan; Reform now affirms full egalitarianism; Regina Jonas was privately ordained in Germany in 1935 - the first woman rabbi, ordained by Rabbi Max Dienemann after the Hochschule für die Wissenschaft des Judentums declined to ordain her - while Sally Priesand became the first woman ordained by a seminary, Reform's Hebrew Union College, in 1972 (the cleaner example of institutional Reform egalitarianism).

whatToKnow.point: WORK FROM A PRACTICE - Shabbat (the Sabbath) makes the belief difference concrete and observable
whatToKnow.detail: Shabbat rests on Exodus 20:8, 'Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy,' and Genesis 2:2–3. Orthodox practice keeps the 39 categories of melakhah (forbidden creative work, from the Mishnah tractate Shabbat) - no driving, no switching on electricity, no carrying outside an eruv, no writing or handling money; men and women sit separately (mechitzah); the service is entirely in Hebrew. Reform, by autonomy, often permits driving to synagogue (formalised by an American Conservative responsum in 1950 but characteristic of liberal practice), uses electricity, holds mixed-gender seating, includes English alongside Hebrew, and may use instrumental music/organ - the very organ that sparked the 1818–1819 controversy. Same commandment, opposite reading of what 'keeping it holy' requires.

whatToKnow.point: Conflict has been real and institutional, not merely a difference of style
whatToKnow.detail: The Hamburg Temple prayerbook (1819) drew a rabbinic ban (the pamphlet 'Eleh Divrei haBrit'). In 1883 the 'Trefa Banquet' at Hebrew Union College in Cincinnati - shrimp and other non-kosher food served at the ordination dinner - outraged traditionalists and helped trigger the founding of the Conservative movement's Jewish Theological Seminary (1886). The deepest modern conflict is over the 'Who is a Jew?' question and personal status: Orthodoxy does not recognise Reform conversions or Reform-officiated marriages/divorces, and Israel's Chief Rabbinate (Orthodox) controls official marriage, so Reform Jews and converts face non-recognition. Reform accepts PATRILINEAL descent (1983 CCAR resolution); Orthodoxy holds Jewishness passes only matrilineally - a direct, ongoing clash.

whatToKnow.point: Cooperation is equally real - shared peoplehood, defence, and Israel
whatToKnow.detail: Across the divide both are recognisably one people (klal Yisrael / am Yisrael). They cooperate through umbrella and defence bodies - e.g. the Board of Deputies of British Jews, the Conference of Presidents of Major American Jewish Organizations, and locally the Executive Council of Australian Jewry - on antisemitism, security, Holocaust memory (Yom HaShoah), and support for the State of Israel. Interdenominational chevra kadisha (burial societies), kosher/communal infrastructure and joint charity (tzedakah) are often shared. The shared baseline beliefs remain: one God (the Shema, Deuteronomy 6:4), the Torah's centrality, and a common festival calendar (Pesach, Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah).

pointsOfView.label: Orthodox self-understanding
pointsOfView.who: Modern Orthodox and Haredi Judaism (e.g. Samson Raphael Hirsch's Torah im Derekh Eretz; the Chatam Sofer's traditionalism)
pointsOfView.summary: The Torah, written and oral, was given by God at Sinai and is eternally binding; halakhah is not negotiable. 'The new is forbidden by the Torah.' Modern Orthodoxy (Hirsch, later Joseph B. Soloveitchik) engages the modern world while keeping full halakhic observance; Haredi Orthodoxy minimises accommodation. Reform is seen as having abandoned binding law.

pointsOfView.label: Reform/Progressive self-understanding
pointsOfView.who: Reform Judaism (Abraham Geiger; CCAR - Central Conference of American Rabbis)
pointsOfView.summary: Judaism is a living, evolving faith; revelation is progressive and each generation reinterprets Torah through reason and conscience. Ethical monotheism and the prophetic 'mission to humanity' are the heart; ritual is meaningful but not coercive. Autonomy of the individual Jew is central. Geiger argued Judaism had always changed historically.

pointsOfView.label: The 'middle' position that frames the binary
pointsOfView.who: Conservative (Masorti) Judaism (Zecharias Frankel; Solomon Schechter, JTS)
pointsOfView.summary: Founded as 'positive-historical Judaism' between the two: halakhah IS binding and authoritative, but it has always developed historically and can evolve through proper rabbinic process. Useful in an essay to show Orthodox vs Reform is a spectrum, not a clean pair - but the question asks for TWO variants, so keep Conservative as context.

pointsOfView.label: Australian/Tasmanian context
pointsOfView.who: Australian Jewry (Orthodox-majority communally; Progressive Judaism via the UPJ - Union for Progressive Judaism)
pointsOfView.summary: Australian Jewish communal life is historically Orthodox-led (the major synagogue movements and the Sydney/Melbourne Beth Din are Orthodox), with the Union for Progressive Judaism representing Reform/Progressive congregations. Tasmania has the Hobart Synagogue (1845) and Launceston Synagogue (1846) - the oldest in Australia - useful concrete local anchors.

sources.reference: Exodus 20:8 (KJV/public domain): 'Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.'
sources.useFor: Anchoring the Shabbat 'work from a practice' section; the shared command both variants interpret differently.
sources.snippet: Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.

sources.reference: Deuteronomy 6:4 (the Shema): 'Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one.'
sources.useFor: The shared belief (monotheism) that grounds the cooperation/klal Yisrael paragraph.
sources.snippet: Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one.

sources.reference: Pittsburgh Platform (1885), Central Conference of American Rabbis (public domain founding statement of Reform)
sources.useFor: Direct evidence of Reform rejecting binding ritual/dietary law and reframing Messiah as a Messianic age.
sources.snippet: Reform declared Mosaic and rabbinical ritual laws not adapted to modern civilisation no longer binding, keeping only moral laws.

sources.reference: Mishnah, Tractate Shabbat 7:2 - the list of 39 melakhot (categories of forbidden work)
sources.useFor: Specifying exactly what Orthodox Shabbat observance prohibits; concrete practice detail.
sources.snippet: The 39 primary categories of creative work forbidden on Shabbat (e.g. kindling fire, writing, carrying).

sources.reference: Nicholas de Lange, 'An Introduction to Judaism' (Cambridge)
sources.useFor: Reliable overview of the denominations, Emancipation and Geiger; paraphrase only.
sources.snippet: Traces Reform to Enlightenment-era Germany and the authority-of-halakhah question.

sources.reference: Michael A. Meyer, 'Response to Modernity: A History of the Reform Movement in Judaism'
sources.useFor: Authoritative on Jacobson, Hamburg Temple, the synods and the Pittsburgh Platform.
sources.snippet: Documents Reform's institutional origins and its theological self-definition.

sources.reference: Union for Progressive Judaism (Australia) and Executive Council of Australian Jewry public materials
sources.useFor: Australian/Tasmanian localisation and the cooperation paragraph.
sources.snippet: Progressive congregations under the UPJ; ECAJ as the cross-denominational roof body.

A-variant|Judaism.workedThesis: While Orthodox and Reform Judaism share one God, one Torah and one people, their split - born of nineteenth-century German Emancipation - turns on a single decisive question: whether halakhah is the unchanging command of God or an evolving human response to it; this disagreement is most significant because it reshapes not only what Jews believe about revelation, but observably how they keep the Sabbath, who they recognise as a Jew, and yet it has never broken the deeper bond of klal Yisrael.
paragraphPlan: Intro: define the two variants accurately (Orthodox = halakhah as binding divine revelation; Reform/Progressive = progressive revelation + autonomy), name the German-Emancipation origin, and state the thesis that the halakhah-authority question is the most significant difference.

Origin paragraph: explain the split - Haskalah/Emancipation, Jacobson and the Hamburg Temple (1818), Reform synods (1844–46), and Orthodoxy crystallising in reaction via the Chatam Sofer's 'the new is forbidden by the Torah.'

Belief paragraph (ANALYSE): trace how the authority question produces concrete belief differences - Torah min haShamayim vs progressive revelation; personal Messiah/Temple vs Messianic age; matrilineal vs patrilineal descent (1983).

Practice paragraph WORKED FROM SHABBAT (ANALYSE→EVALUATE): Exodus 20:8 read two ways - Orthodox 39 melakhot, mechitzah, all-Hebrew, no driving/electricity vs Reform driving, electricity, mixed seating, organ/English; show the practice is the belief made visible.

Conflict AND cooperation paragraph (EVALUATE): Trefa Banquet (1883), 'Who is a Jew?', the Israeli Chief Rabbinate's non-recognition of Reform conversions/marriage - set against klal Yisrael, the Shema, shared festivals, and joint bodies (ECAJ, Board of Deputies) defending against antisemitism.

Conclusion: weigh significance - the divide is deep and institutional yet bounded by shared peoplehood; judge that the halakhah question matters most because it is simultaneously theological and lived.
A-variant|Judaism.exemplarParagraph: The most revealing place to weigh the significance of the Orthodox–Reform divide is not a creed but a practice: the keeping of the Sabbath, commanded in Exodus 20:8, 'Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.' Because Orthodoxy holds that the Torah's law is God's unchanging command, 'keeping it holy' means observing the thirty-nine categories of forbidden creative work (melakhah) listed in the Mishnah's tractate Shabbat - so an Orthodox Jew will not drive, switch on electricity, write or handle money, will worship entirely in Hebrew, and will sit behind a mechitzah dividing men and women. Reform Judaism reads the very same verse through progressive revelation and personal autonomy, concluding that the Sabbath's holiness lies in rest and renewal rather than in the detail of ancient labour categories; hence many Reform Jews drive to synagogue, use electricity, sit in mixed-gender pews, pray in both English and Hebrew, and welcome the organ - the same instrument whose introduction at the Hamburg Temple in 1818 had first scandalised the traditionalists. The significance of this is that the practice makes the theology visible and testable: the abstract argument over whether halakhah is divine command or human interpretation is not academic, because it decides what a Jew actually does on a Saturday morning. Yet it would be an error to overstate the rupture - both are unmistakably keeping the Sabbath, both anchor it in the same Torah verse and the same God of the Shema - which is precisely why the difference is best judged as profound in expression but bounded by a shared inheritance, a division within one people rather than between two religions.

ladderForThis.C: DESCRIBE/EXPLAIN: State accurately what each variant is and where it came from - e.g. 'Reform Judaism began in early-1800s Germany after Emancipation; Israel Jacobson introduced organ and German prayers, the Hamburg Temple opened in 1818. Orthodoxy holds the Torah was given at Sinai and halakhah is binding; the Chatam Sofer said the new is forbidden by the Torah. Describe one belief (Messiah) and one practice (Shabbat) for each.'
ladderForThis.B: ANALYSE: Show CAUSE AND CONNECTION - explain how the single disagreement over the authority of halakhah PRODUCES the downstream differences. 'Because Reform treats revelation as progressive and law as a matter of autonomy, it permits driving and mixed seating on Shabbat and accepts patrilineal descent; because Orthodoxy treats halakhah as fixed divine command, it keeps the 39 melakhot and matrilineal descent. The belief difference and the practice difference are the same difference seen at two levels.'
ladderForThis.A: EVALUATE / CRITICALLY ANALYSE: Make and defend a JUDGEMENT about significance, weighing conflict against cooperation. 'The halakhah-authority question is the MOST significant difference because it is simultaneously theological and lived - it explains both the deepest conflict (the Chief Rabbinate refusing to recognise Reform conversions and marriages, the Who-is-a-Jew dispute) and the limits of that conflict (shared monotheism, Torah, festivals and defence bodies like the ECAJ). Conclude that the divide is profound in expression yet bounded by klal Yisrael - a split within one people, not into two faiths.'

trapsForThisQuestion: DO NOT use Christian vocabulary for Jewish things - never 'chapel', 'church service', 'congregation worships at chapel', 'priest', or 'communion'. Use synagogue (or temple/shul), service, rabbi, congregation.

DO NOT say 'Orthodox Jews are the strict ones and Reform are the relaxed ones' as if it's about effort - frame it accurately as a disagreement over the AUTHORITY and nature of halakhah, not laziness vs piety.

DO NOT claim ALL Orthodox or ALL Reform Jews do X - say 'Orthodox practice typically/halakhically requires…' and 'many Reform Jews…'. Reform's principle is autonomy, so practice varies by individual and congregation.

DO NOT confuse Reform with Conservative (Masorti). Conservative/Masorti is a SEPARATE middle movement (Frankel, Schechter, JTS) that keeps halakhah as binding-but-evolving. Driving-to-synagogue was a 1950 CONSERVATIVE responsum (a permissive concession for those living far from a synagogue, not a general endorsement of driving on Shabbat) - attribute it correctly if you mention it.
DO NOT date the split to antiquity or to Jesus - it is a 19th-century, post-Emancipation, German development. (Pharisees/Sadducees are a different, ancient split - don't muddle them in.)
DO NOT say Reform 'rejected the Torah' or 'stopped being Jewish' - they reinterpret Torah as divinely inspired and progressively revealed; they keep ethical mitzvot and the festival calendar.

DO NOT forget the COOPERATION half - an A-grade answer must weigh shared peoplehood (klal Yisrael, the Shema, joint defence bodies like the ECAJ/Board of Deputies) against the conflict, not just list disagreements.

DO NOT muddle matrilineal vs patrilineal: Orthodoxy = Jewishness passes through the mother (matrilineal); Reform's 1983 CCAR resolution accepts patrilineal descent too. Getting this backwards is a penalised factual error.

B-suffering|Christianity.topic: Section B - Suffering: how Christianity's understanding of human nature and purpose shapes its understanding of suffering and its responses to suffering (case study response).

whatToKnow.point: Christian human nature: imago Dei + the Fall set the frame for suffering
whatToKnow.detail: Genesis 1:27 says humans are made 'in the image of God' (imago Dei), giving every person dignity and a relationship-with-God purpose. Genesis 3 (the Fall - Adam and Eve) introduces sin, death and disordered creation, so most Christian traditions read suffering as a consequence of a fallen world rather than God's original design. Augustine of Hippo (354–430), in 'Confessions' and 'City of God', argues evil is not a created 'thing' but a privatio boni - a privation/corruption of a good creation; this underwrites the classic free-will defence.

whatToKnow.point: Purpose of human life reframes suffering as meaningful, not merely punitive
whatToKnow.detail: If the human telos is union/relationship with God (Westminster Shorter Catechism Q1: humanity's chief end is 'to glorify God, and to enjoy him forever'), suffering can be redemptive - a means of growth, solidarity with Christ, or 'soul-making'. Irenaeus of Lyons (c.130–202) frames creation as a process of maturing toward God's likeness; the modern philosopher John Hick (1922–2012) develops this into the 'soul-making theodicy' (Evil and the God of Love, 1966). Romans 5:3–4: 'suffering produces perseverance; perseverance, character; and character, hope.'

whatToKnow.point: The cross is the centre: God suffers WITH humanity (Christology, not just explanation)
whatToKnow.detail: Christianity's distinctive move is that suffering is not only explained but entered - the incarnate Son suffers and dies (the crucifixion). Jürgen Moltmann's 'The Crucified God' (1972/1974) argues God is not the impassive 'apathetic' deity of Greek thought but co-suffers; the cross means God is present in Godforsakenness (Jesus' cry, Mark 15:34, 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'). This shapes responses: Christians are called to imitate Christ in compassion (literally 'suffering-with').

whatToKnow.point: Variant differences are real and examinable - do not flatten 'Christianity'
whatToKnow.detail: Roman Catholicism affirms redemptive suffering 'offered up' in union with Christ (John Paul II, apostolic letter Salvifici Doloris, 1984) and a sacramental response (Anointing of the Sick). Eastern Orthodoxy stresses theosis and reads the Fall less as inherited guilt than as inherited mortality/corruption, so suffering is healing toward deification. Many Protestants (e.g. Reformed/Calvinist) emphasise God's sovereignty and providence (Romans 8:28 - God works 'for the good'); Lutherans stress the 'theology of the cross' (Luther, Heidelberg Disputation, 1518). NOTE accuracy: Anglicans are typically classed within the Reformation/Protestant family but self-describe as via media; do not call Catholics or Orthodox 'Protestant'.

whatToKnow.point: The problem of evil is the sharpest challenge - and Christian responses to it
whatToKnow.detail: The problem of evil - classically the trilemma formulated by Epicurus, transmitted via Lactantius (De Ira Dei), and popularised by David Hume (Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion, 1779) - asks how an omnipotent, omnibenevolent God permits evil. Christian replies: the free-will defence (Alvin Plantinga, God, Freedom, and Evil, 1974) for moral evil; soul-making (Hick) for the value of struggle; eschatological hope (Revelation 21:4 - God 'will wipe every tear', no more death) for the not-yet. The Book of Job rejects simple retribution theology: Job suffers though righteous, and God's answer (Job 38–42) reframes the question rather than giving a tidy cause.

whatToKnow.point: CASE STUDY (response, not history): Mother Teresa's Missionaries of Charity as a lived response - with safe alternatives
whatToKnow.detail: Best teachable case for 'response to suffering' is the Missionaries of Charity (founded by Mother Teresa, Calcutta, 1950): they respond to the suffering poor and dying by seeing Christ in 'the distressing disguise of the poor' (Matthew 25:40 - 'whatever you did for one of the least of these... you did for me'). The response is not to explain away suffering but to accompany, dignify and care - embodying compassion and imago Dei. Note honestly: published letters (Come Be My Light, 2007) reveal Teresa's own decades of spiritual darkness, which itself illustrates redemptive/co-suffering theology rather than undermining it. (Alternative safe cases: Dietrich Bonhoeffer's resistance and 'costly grace', or the modern hospice movement / Cicely Saunders, founder of St Christopher's Hospice 1967, a Christian response to dying.)

pointsOfView.label: Augustinian / free-will (Western)
pointsOfView.who: Augustine of Hippo; later Plantinga
pointsOfView.summary: Suffering and evil stem from the misuse of free will in a fallen world; evil is privation of good, not a created thing. God permits it because freely-chosen love is a greater good. Dominant in Catholic and much Protestant thought.

pointsOfView.label: Irenaean / soul-making
pointsOfView.who: Irenaeus of Lyons; John Hick (20th c.)
pointsOfView.summary: Humans are created immature and grow toward God's likeness THROUGH a world containing genuine hardship; suffering can build 'souls'. Treats the world as a 'vale of soul-making', not a paradise gone wrong.

pointsOfView.label: Theology of the cross / co-suffering God
pointsOfView.who: Martin Luther (1518); Jürgen Moltmann (1972)
pointsOfView.summary: God is known not in glory but in the crucified Christ; God suffers with creation. The response to suffering is solidarity and compassion, not tidy explanation. Strong in Lutheran and modern political theology.

pointsOfView.label: Eastern Orthodox / theosis
pointsOfView.who: Orthodox tradition; Maximus the Confessor, Gregory of Nyssa
pointsOfView.summary: The Fall brought mortality and corruption more than inherited guilt; suffering is part of healing and deification (theosis). God's response is therapeutic - the Church as a 'hospital' for souls.

pointsOfView.label: Reformed providence
pointsOfView.who: John Calvin; Westminster Confession (1646)
pointsOfView.summary: God's sovereign providence governs all events; even suffering serves God's good purposes (Romans 8:28). Comfort lies in trust, not in fully understanding why.

pointsOfView.label: Catholic redemptive suffering (lived)
pointsOfView.who: Pope John Paul II, Salvifici Doloris (1984); Mother Teresa
pointsOfView.summary: Human suffering, united to Christ's, has saving value and can be 'offered up'; the proper response is compassionate accompaniment of the suffering, seeing Christ in them.

sources.reference: Genesis 1:27; Genesis 3 (KJV/NRSV, public-domain KJV)
sources.useFor: imago Dei and the Fall - the human-nature frame for suffering
sources.snippet: 'So God created man in his own image' (Gen 1:27).

sources.reference: Book of Job, esp. Job 1–2; 38–42
sources.useFor: rejection of simple retribution theology; God reframes the why of innocent suffering
sources.snippet: The righteous Job suffers; God answers from the whirlwind rather than itemising a cause.

sources.reference: Romans 5:3–4; Romans 8:28
sources.useFor: redemptive suffering and providence
sources.snippet: 'suffering produces perseverance... character... hope'; God 'works for the good of those who love him'.

sources.reference: Mark 15:34
sources.useFor: the cry of dereliction - God-in-Christ enters Godforsakenness
sources.snippet: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'

sources.reference: Matthew 25:40; Revelation 21:4
sources.useFor: case-study warrant (serve Christ in the suffering) and eschatological hope
sources.snippet: 'whatever you did for one of the least of these... you did for me'; 'he will wipe every tear'.

sources.reference: John Hick, Evil and the God of Love (1966)
sources.useFor: soul-making theodicy - paraphrase, do not quote at length
sources.snippet: World as a 'vale of soul-making' that matures persons toward God.

sources.reference: Jürgen Moltmann, The Crucified God (1972/74)
sources.useFor: the co-suffering, passible God; response = solidarity
sources.snippet: God is revealed in the crucified Christ, suffering with the godforsaken.

sources.reference: Pope John Paul II, Salvifici Doloris (1984)
sources.useFor: Catholic doctrine of redemptive suffering for the case study
sources.snippet: Human suffering united to Christ's gains salvific meaning.

sources.reference: Alvin Plantinga, God, Freedom, and Evil (1974)
sources.useFor: the modern free-will defence against the logical problem of evil
sources.snippet: A world with free creatures who sometimes choose wrongly can be better than one without freedom.

sources.reference: Mother Teresa, Come Be My Light (ed. Kolodiejchuk, 2007)
sources.useFor: case study: lived response + Teresa's own 'darkness' as co-suffering
sources.snippet: Decades of interior spiritual darkness alongside service to the dying poor.

B-suffering|Christianity.workedThesis: Because Christianity understands human beings as made in God's image yet living in a fallen world whose purpose is union with God, it interprets suffering not as meaningless but as something to be redeemed and entered - a reading that finds its decisive warrant in the cross and is most significantly embodied in responses like Mother Teresa's Missionaries of Charity, where suffering is met with compassionate accompaniment rather than explanation.
paragraphPlan: Intro: state the human-nature frame (imago Dei + Fall + telos of union with God) and thesis that this frame drives BOTH understanding AND response to suffering; flag variant range.

Body 1 (C/B): explain the human-nature foundation - Genesis 1:27 imago Dei gives dignity; Genesis 3 Fall explains a disordered world; Augustine's privatio boni and the free-will defence. Analyse how this makes suffering intelligible but not God-authored.

Body 2 (B): analyse the purpose dimension - telos of union with God makes suffering potentially redemptive; contrast Irenaean soul-making (Hick) with Augustinian free-will; bring in Job and Romans 5 to show suffering reframed as formative, not simply punitive.

Body 3 (A): EVALUATE the significance of the cross - Moltmann's crucified God and Luther's theology of the cross; argue the distinctively Christian contribution is a co-suffering God, weigh this against the unresolved problem of evil (Epicurus/Hume, Plantinga's reply, eschatology of Rev 21:4).

Body 4 (A, case study): the Missionaries of Charity as the response embodied - Matthew 25:40 warrant, accompaniment-not-explanation, Teresa's own darkness as living co-suffering; evaluate how significantly this case demonstrates the doctrine in practice, and note variant agreement/tension (Catholic redemptive suffering vs Reformed providence).

Conclusion: judge that the human-nature/purpose frame is what gives Christian suffering-responses their coherence - significance lies in turning an explanatory problem into a vocation of compassion, while honestly conceding the problem of evil is mitigated, not dissolved.
B-suffering|Christianity.exemplarParagraph: The most significant feature of the Christian understanding of suffering is not any single explanation but the claim that God enters suffering rather than merely permitting it, and the weight of this claim is best measured at the cross. Jürgen Moltmann, in The Crucified God (1972), argues that Jesus' cry of dereliction - 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Mark 15:34) - reveals a God who is not the detached, impassible deity of Greek philosophy but one who co-suffers with the godforsaken; Martin Luther had earlier captured this in his 1518 'theology of the cross', insisting God is known in weakness and suffering, not in worldly glory. This matters because it transforms the logic of the whole question: where the Augustinian free-will defence and Hick's soul-making theodicy try to justify suffering, the cross instead joins God to it, so that the human purpose of union with God is fulfilled precisely through, not despite, affliction (Romans 5:3–4). Its significance, however, must be evaluated honestly rather than overstated. The cross does not dissolve the problem of evil that Epicurus and Hume sharpened - it offers solidarity, not a tidy answer, and Plantinga's free-will defence and the eschatological promise that God 'will wipe every tear' (Revelation 21:4) remain logically necessary supplements. Yet this is arguably the point: by relocating the response from explanation to presence, Christianity makes its account of human nature - fallen yet imago Dei, destined for God - coherent in a way no abstract theodicy alone achieves, which is why the tradition's most powerful answers to suffering are lived rather than argued.

ladderForThis.C: Describe/explain: state that Christianity sees humans as imago Dei (Gen 1:27) living in a fallen world (Gen 3), with the purpose of union with God; explain that this makes suffering a consequence of the Fall and potentially redemptive, and describe one response (e.g. Missionaries of Charity caring for the dying).
ladderForThis.B: Analyse: show HOW the human-nature beliefs cause the responses - e.g. connect imago Dei to the dignity-driven care of the poor, link the telos of union with God to soul-making (Hick) vs free-will (Augustine), and contrast at least two variants (Catholic redemptive suffering vs Reformed providence vs Orthodox theosis) so 'Christianity' is not flattened.
ladderForThis.A: Evaluate significance / critically analyse: judge HOW SIGNIFICANTLY the cross-centred, co-suffering frame (Moltmann/Luther) shapes responses; weigh its strength (turns explanation into compassionate vocation, e.g. Mother Teresa) against its limit (does not solve the problem of evil - Epicurus/Hume, only mitigated by Plantinga + eschatology), and reach a defended judgement about what makes the Christian response distinctive.

trapsForThisQuestion: Flattening 'Christianity' into one view - examiners reward naming variants (Catholic redemptive suffering / Reformed providence / Orthodox theosis / Lutheran theology of the cross) and penalise 'Christians believe...' as if monolithic.
Calling Roman Catholics or Eastern Orthodox 'Protestant', or treating Anglicans loosely - be precise: Catholic and Orthodox are NOT Protestant; Anglicans sit in the Reformation family as a via media.
Only EXPLAINING when the verb is EVALUATE - for an A you must judge the SIGNIFICANCE of the human-nature frame and the cross, not just describe beliefs.
Doing the HISTORY of the case study instead of the RESPONSE - don't narrate Mother Teresa's biography; analyse how the Missionaries of Charity's care embodies imago Dei and Matthew 25:40.
Presenting theodicy as if it 'solves' the problem of evil - honest A-grade answers concede the cross offers solidarity/hope, not proof; overclaiming is penalised.
Misusing the Book of Job as proof that suffering is punishment - Job is the OPPOSITE: it rejects retribution theology (the righteous suffer; the friends are rebuked).
Confusing the two main theodicies - Augustinian = free-will/Fall/privation looking BACK; Irenaean/Hick = soul-making looking FORWARD; mixing them loses analytic marks.
Forgetting eschatology - leaving out Revelation 21:4 / the 'not-yet' makes the Christian response look weaker than the tradition actually holds.

B-suffering|Judaism.topic: Section B - Religious responses to suffering: how Judaism's understanding of human nature and purpose shapes its understanding of, and responses to, suffering (with a case-study illustration)

whatToKnow.point: Human nature in Judaism: the yetzer ha-tov and yetzer ha-ra, and the body-soul unity - humans are not fallen but are made b'tzelem Elohim (in the image of God).
whatToKnow.detail: Genesis 1:27 states humanity is created 'b'tzelem Elohim' (in the image of God), giving every person inherent dignity and moral capacity. Rabbinic anthropology (Talmud, Berakhot 61a; Genesis Rabbah) holds that each person has two inclinations - the yetzer ha-tov (inclination to good) and yetzer ha-ra (inclination to evil/self-interest), which is also the drive behind ambition, work and procreation. Crucially Judaism has NO doctrine of original sin (contrast Christianity): Deuteronomy 30:19 - 'I have set before you life and death... therefore choose life' - affirms free will and human responsibility. This shapes suffering: because humans are responsible moral agents in a covenant, suffering is met by ACTION (mitzvot, repair) rather than by waiting for grace.

whatToKnow.point: Human purpose: covenant, tikkun olam (repair of the world), and partnership with God - purpose is to sanctify life and act, which makes suffering something to be responded to, not merely endured.
whatToKnow.detail: The human purpose in Judaism is to keep the covenant (brit) given at Sinai and to live by the mitzvot (613 commandments), partnering with God in completing/repairing creation - tikkun olam. The Aleinu prayer's phrase 'l'takken olam' frames Jews as God's partners in mending the world. Because purpose is active world-repair and life-sanctification (the principle of pikuach nefesh - saving a life overrides almost all commandments, Talmud Yoma 85b), suffering is not glorified or sought; it is a condition to be alleviated through deeds. This directly drives the response: gemilut chasadim (acts of loving-kindness), tzedakah (justice/charity), and bikkur cholim (visiting the sick).

whatToKnow.point: Understandings of WHY suffering exists: multiple, non-uniform Jewish frameworks - there is no single official 'answer' (theodicy is debated, not settled).
whatToKnow.detail: Judaism holds several models in tension: (1) suffering as consequence/discipline within the covenant - the Deuteronomic 'blessings and curses' (Deuteronomy 28); (2) yissurin shel ahavah, 'afflictions of love' that refine the righteous (Talmud, Berakhot 5a); (3) the PROTEST tradition - Abraham challenges God ('Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?' Genesis 18:25), and above all the Book of Job, where God rejects the friends' neat retribution theology (Job 42:7) and the problem is left as mystery; (4) Ecclesiastes/Kohelet's acceptance of life's hevel ('vanity/breath'). A student must show suffering's MEANING is genuinely contested in Judaism, not resolved - showing the live spread of positions (refinement/yissurin shel ahavah vs Job's anti-theodicy vs Rubinstein's break with the God of history) is what earns the higher criteria.

whatToKnow.point: Practices that channel the response to suffering - these must be LINKED to suffering, not just described (the examiner's key warning).
whatToKnow.detail: Specific practices: (a) mourning rites - shiva (seven days), sheloshim (thirty days), tearing the garment (keriah), and the Mourner's Kaddish (which praises God and never mentions death, asserting meaning amid loss); (b) the bracha on hearing of a death - 'Baruch Dayan ha-Emet' ('Blessed is the True Judge'); (c) bikkur cholim (visiting the sick) and the community minyan; (d) Shabbat and prayer as solace; (e) the value of comforting mourners (nichum aveilim). Examiners explicitly note candidates must TRANSITION from describing a practice (e.g. Shabbat) to HOW it guides an adherent through suffering - that link earns Criterion 7.

whatToKnow.point: The CASE STUDY: the Jewish response to the suffering of the Shoah (Holocaust) - the RESPONSE, not the history. (Examiners penalise Holocaust-history recounts.)
whatToKnow.detail: The case study illustrates how Jewish belief/value/practice helped adherents understand and cope (or struggle) with suffering. Key responses: (1) Kiddush ha-Shem (sanctification of God's Name) - dying as a Jew with faith; some continued mitzvot in the camps. (2) Kiddush ha-Chayim - Rabbi Shimon Huberband and others documented and embodied SANCTIFYING LIFE (survival, preserving humanity) as itself the holy response. (3) Post-Shoah theology: Richard Rubinstein (After Auschwitz, 1966) argued the covenantal God of history was dead; Emil Fackenheim countered with the '614th commandment' - Jews are forbidden to grant Hitler a posthumous victory, i.e. they MUST survive as Jews; Eliezer Berkovits (Faith After the Holocaust, 1973) used hester panim ('the hiding of God's face') and human free will to preserve faith; Ignaz Maybaum and others offered differing readings. Elie Wiesel's Night dramatises protest-faith. The case study's job: show these as RESPONSES flowing from human-nature/purpose beliefs.

whatToKnow.point: Variant differences (Orthodox / Conservative-Masorti / Reform / Haredi) - accuracy matters; do NOT say 'all Jews believe X'.
whatToKnow.detail: Orthodox and especially Haredi thought tends to retain robust traditional theodicy (suffering within divine providence, hashgachah pratit; some controversial Haredi figures even framed the Shoah in punishment terms, which most Jews reject). Reform Judaism (and much Conservative thought) leans toward human-freedom and protest models, emphasising tikkun olam and ethical action over claims that God wills suffering. Many post-Shoah Jews became theologically agnostic about WHY while remaining committed to the RESPONSE (peoplehood, memory - Yom HaShoah, 'Never Again'). Examiners reward showing this internal range rather than a monolithic 'Judaism says'.

pointsOfView.label: Afflictions of love / refinement
pointsOfView.who: Classical Rabbinic (Talmud, Berakhot 5a) - retained in much Orthodox thought
pointsOfView.summary: Suffering can be 'yissurin shel ahavah' - afflictions of love that God sends to refine and draw close the righteous, expiating sin and increasing reward in the world to come. Suffering is meaningful within divine providence.

pointsOfView.label: Protest / mystery (anti-theodicy)
pointsOfView.who: The Book of Job; the Abraham-bargains-for-Sodom tradition; Elie Wiesel; much modern Reform/Conservative thought
pointsOfView.summary: Suffering, especially of the innocent, is NOT neatly explained. Job's comforters are rebuked (Job 42:7); the faithful response is honest protest WITHIN relationship to God ('arguing with God'), not pious justification. Meaning is found in fidelity and action, not in an answer.

pointsOfView.who: Emil Fackenheim (Orthodox-influenced, post-Shoah)
pointsOfView.label: The 614th commandment
pointsOfView.summary: After Auschwitz, Jews are commanded not to hand Hitler a posthumous victory: to survive as Jews, remember, and not despair of God or humanity. Purpose (covenantal survival) becomes the response to suffering itself.

pointsOfView.who: Richard Rubinstein (radical, After Auschwitz 1966)
pointsOfView.label: Death of the covenantal God of history
pointsOfView.summary: The traditional God who acts in history cannot be maintained after the Shoah; Rubinstein moves toward a naturalistic Judaism centred on peoplehood and ritual rather than a God who wills or permits such suffering - a minority but influential position.

pointsOfView.who: Eliezer Berkovits (Orthodox, Faith After the Holocaust 1973)
pointsOfView.label: Hester panim + free will
pointsOfView.summary: God 'hides his face' (hester panim) to allow genuine human freedom; the Shoah is the catastrophic cost of the free will that makes humans moral agents, not divine punishment. Faith and the covenant survive intact.

sources.reference: Genesis 1:27 (Tanakh) - 'God created humankind in His image (b'tzelem Elohim).'
sources.useFor: Establishing the human-nature premise: inherent dignity, moral agency, no original sin - the root of why Judaism responds to suffering with action.
sources.snippet: 'So God created humankind in His image, in the image of God He created them.'

sources.reference: Deuteronomy 30:19 (Tanakh)
sources.useFor: Free will and human responsibility - purpose is to 'choose life', so suffering is to be alleviated not passively accepted.
sources.snippet: 'I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse; therefore choose life.'

sources.reference: Genesis 18:25 (Tanakh) - Abraham pleads for Sodom
sources.useFor: The protest tradition / arguing with God as a legitimate response to suffering and injustice.
sources.snippet: 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?'

sources.reference: Book of Job, esp. Job 42:7 (Tanakh)
sources.useFor: The anti-theodicy / mystery position; God rebukes the friends' tidy retribution theology - meaning of suffering is left open.
sources.snippet: God's anger is against the friends, 'for you have not spoken of Me what is right.'

sources.reference: Talmud Bavli, Berakhot 5a and Yoma 85b
sources.useFor: Berakhot 5a = 'afflictions of love' (yissurin shel ahavah); Yoma 85b = pikuach nefesh (saving life overrides commandments) - shows life-sanctifying purpose driving the response.
sources.snippet: Suffering may be 'chastisements of love'; and 'the Sabbath is given to you, not you to the Sabbath' - saving life overrides it.

sources.reference: Emil Fackenheim, God's Presence in History (1970) - the 614th commandment (first stated in his 1967 essay 'Jewish Faith and the Holocaust')
sources.useFor: Case-study response: survival-as-mitzvah after the Shoah; links purpose (covenantal continuity) to the response to suffering.
sources.snippet: Jews are forbidden to grant Hitler posthumous victories; they must survive as Jews and not despair.

sources.reference: Eliezer Berkovits, Faith After the Holocaust (1973)
sources.useFor: Variant theological response: hester panim + free will preserves faith without blaming the victims.
sources.snippet: God's hiddenness is the price of the human freedom that makes moral life possible.

sources.reference: Richard Rubinstein, After Auschwitz (1966)
sources.useFor: The radical minority response - used to demonstrate genuine internal diversity: refinement/providence vs protest vs Rubinstein's break with the God of history.
sources.snippet: The God of covenant-history is no longer tenable; Judaism re-centres on peoplehood.

sources.reference: Mourner's Kaddish and the blessing 'Baruch Dayan ha-Emet'
sources.useFor: Practice linked to suffering: the Kaddish praises God without mentioning death; 'Blessed is the True Judge' is said on hearing of a death - affirming meaning amid grief.
sources.snippet: 'Blessed is the True Judge' - recited at the moment of loss.

B-suffering|Judaism.workedThesis: Because Judaism understands the human being as created in the image of God, free, responsible and without original sin, and understands human purpose as covenantal partnership in repairing the world (tikkun olam), it treats suffering not as a state to be passively endured or piously justified but as a condition to be wrestled with and actively alleviated - so that its most significant 'response' to suffering is less a single theodicy than a coherent insistence on protest, memory and life-affirming action, as the diverse post-Shoah responses make clear.
paragraphPlan: Introduction: name the contention - human-nature/purpose beliefs (image of God, free will, no original sin, covenant/tikkun olam) DRIVE both how Judaism understands suffering and how it responds; signpost that the response is action + contested meaning, illustrated by the Shoah case study; flag internal diversity.

Body 1 (human nature → understanding): b'tzelem Elohim, yetzer ha-tov/ha-ra, no original sin, free will (Deut 30:19). EVALUATE significance: because humans are responsible agents not fallen creatures, suffering becomes a moral problem to be answered by deeds, not by waiting for grace - contrast Christianity to sharpen the point.

Body 2 (human purpose → understanding): covenant, mitzvot, tikkun olam, pikuach nefesh. Show purpose (sanctify and repair life) explains WHY Judaism refuses to glorify suffering and prioritises alleviation; evaluate how significant this is in distinguishing Judaism's stance.

Body 3 (contested MEANING of suffering): set the refinement model (yissurin shel ahavah, Berakhot 5a; Deut 28) AGAINST the protest/mystery model (Job 42:7; Abraham, Gen 18:25). Evaluate that the SIGNIFICANCE lies precisely in Judaism holding no single official answer - anti-theodicy is itself a faithful response.

Body 4 (practices as response - the C7 link): shiva, Kaddish, Baruch Dayan ha-Emet, bikkur cholim, nichum aveilim. Explicitly transition from describing each practice to HOW it gives solace/guidance in suffering; evaluate which provides meaning vs comfort.

Body 5 (CASE STUDY - Shoah RESPONSE, not history): Kiddush ha-Shem / Kiddush ha-Chayim in the camps; then post-Shoah theology - Fackenheim's 614th commandment, Berkovits' hester panim, Rubinstein's radical break, Wiesel's protest. Evaluate the coherence/diversity and tie back to human-nature/purpose.

Conclusion: judge significance - the through-line is that an anthropology of free, dignified, covenantally-purposed humans makes Judaism's defining response to suffering active repair, honest protest and memory ('Never Again'), with the meaning of suffering left as living debate rather than dogma.
B-suffering|Judaism.exemplarParagraph: The deepest significance of Judaism's anthropology emerges not in any tidy explanation of suffering but in its striking willingness to leave the question open, a stance best read as a deliberate anti-theodicy rather than a failure to answer. On one side stands the refinement tradition: the Talmud's 'yissurin shel ahavah' (Berakhot 5a) and the Deuteronomic covenant of blessings and curses (Deut 28) construe suffering as discipline or love within God's providence, a reading retained in much Orthodox thought. Yet Judaism canonises its own rebuttal. The Book of Job dismantles exactly this logic - God's wrath falls on the comforters who defended retributive theology 'for you have not spoken of Me what is right' (Job 42:7) - and Abraham's challenge, 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?' (Gen 18:25), legitimises argument with God as an act of faith, not faithlessness. The significance of this internal contest is considerable: because the human being is made b'tzelem Elohim, free and responsible rather than fallen, the tradition can afford honest protest without collapsing into despair or heresy, since meaning is anchored in covenantal action rather than in winning the theological argument. This is precisely why the post-Shoah responses diverge so sharply yet remain recognisably Jewish - Berkovits preserving faith through hester panim, Fackenheim relocating meaning in the command to survive, Rubinstein abandoning the God of history altogether - and why the examiner-rewarded reading is not 'Judaism teaches that suffering means X' but that Judaism's view of human nature licenses a living, unresolved argument in which fidelity is measured by response rather than by resolution.

ladderForThis.C: EXPLAIN/DESCRIBE: State that Jews believe humans are made in God's image and have free will, and describe responses to suffering (mourning rites like shiva and Kaddish, acts of charity/tzedakah, the Shoah as an example). Correctly name beliefs, values and practices and identify that they shape how adherents act.
ladderForThis.B: ANALYSE: Show the CAUSAL link - analyse HOW the beliefs in free will, covenant and tikkun olam lead to specific responses (e.g. why pikuach nefesh and bikkur cholim follow from a life-sanctifying purpose), and analyse how a practice such as the Mourner's Kaddish actually guides an adherent through grief. Compare at least two views (refinement vs protest).
ladderForThis.A: EVALUATE SIGNIFICANCE / CRITICALLY ANALYSE: Judge the significance of the anthropology - argue that BECAUSE humans are free, dignified and covenantally purposed, Judaism's defining response is active repair and honest protest rather than a settled theodicy; critically weigh the coherence of competing post-Shoah responses (Fackenheim vs Rubinstein vs Berkovits) and reach a justified conclusion about what most powerfully shapes adherents' lives.

trapsForThisQuestion: HOLOCAUST-AS-HISTORY DRIFT (the #1 examiner complaint, both 2024 and 2025): do NOT recount the Nazis' rise to power, ghettos, or camp logistics. The case study is the RESPONSE to suffering - Kiddush ha-Chayim, Fackenheim, Kaddish - not the events. Keep history to a sentence of context at most.

FACTUAL ERRORS examiners flagged: never write that Jews attend 'chapel' (it is a synagogue/shul) or believe in 'reincarnation' as a mainstream tenet (gilgul exists in some Kabbalistic/Hasidic thought but is NOT a standard Jewish answer to suffering - don't present it as 'Judaism believes').
ORIGINAL SIN ERROR: do not import the Christian framework. Judaism has NO doctrine of original sin; humans are not 'fallen'. Getting this wrong destroys the human-nature argument.

MONOLITHIC 'JUDAISM SAYS': avoid 'all Jews believe suffering is...'. Show the spread - Orthodox/Haredi providence vs Reform/Conservative protest-and-action vs radical (Rubinstein) - this is exactly the internal diversity the higher criteria reward.

ANSWERING THE WRONG QUESTION: the 2025 stem asks HOW human nature AND purpose shape the understanding of and response to suffering. Examiners noted 'very few candidates addressed how'. Every paragraph must tie back to human nature/purpose, not just list beliefs.

DESCRIBE-WITHOUT-LINKING (Criterion 7 failure): describing Shabbat or shiva is not enough - you must transition to HOW it provides solace/guidance in suffering. Naming a practice without the suffering link scores C3 but misses C7.

MISLABELLING VARIANTS: be precise - 'Haredi/ultra-Orthodox', 'Modern Orthodox', 'Conservative/Masorti', 'Reform/Progressive'. Don't call Reform 'Orthodox' or treat Hasidic mysticism as mainstream halakhic Judaism.
OVER-QUOTING: only short public-domain Tanakh/Talmud lines (Gen 1:27, Deut 30:19, Job 42:7). Paraphrase Fackenheim, Berkovits, Rubinstein and Wiesel - do not reproduce extended copyrighted text.

B-ethics|Christianity.topic: Contemporary Christian ethics: voluntary euthanasia and physician-assisted dying - the sanctity-of-life belief, the named guidance sources, and the differing denominational responses

whatToKnow.point: The underpinning belief is the sanctity (sacredness) of human life - life as a gift from God, not a possession to be disposed of.
whatToKnow.detail: Grounded in Genesis 1:27 ('So God created humankind in his image, in the image of God he created them'), the imago Dei doctrine: because human life bears God's image, the Church infers that it has intrinsic, inviolable worth (the 'inviolable worth' is the doctrinal inference the tradition draws, not a phrase stated in the verse itself). Reinforced by 1 Corinthians 6:19–20 ('your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit... you are not your own; you were bought with a price') and Job 1:21 ('The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away'). God is understood as the author of life and death (Deuteronomy 32:39). Killing the innocent breaches the command against murder, Exodus 20:13 ('You shall not murder') - numbered the sixth commandment in the Catholic/Lutheran tradition but the fifth in the Reformed/Anglican (and Jewish) numbering, so name it by its content rather than only its number.

whatToKnow.point: Define the issue precisely - euthanasia is not one thing, and examiners reward the distinctions.
whatToKnow.detail: Voluntary active euthanasia = a doctor intentionally ends a patient's life at their request (e.g. lethal injection). Physician-assisted dying/suicide = the doctor provides the means and the patient acts. Passive euthanasia = withholding/withdrawing treatment so the person dies of the underlying condition. Key ethical hinge: the distinction between killing and letting die, and the principle of double effect (giving morphine to relieve pain, foreseeing but not intending death, is permissible). In Australia this is live: Victoria's Voluntary Assisted Dying Act 2017 (operative 2019) was the first state law, and all six states have since legislated VAD schemes. (Be precise: the two territories sit outside the 'six states' - the ACT passed its own VAD law in 2024 after the federal Euthanasia Laws Act 1997, which had blocked territory schemes, was repealed in 2022; the NT had not legislated one. So 'all six states' is not the same as 'everywhere in Australia'.)

whatToKnow.point: The Roman Catholic position is the clearest and most absolute - euthanasia is intrinsically evil.
whatToKnow.detail: Magisterial teaching: Catechism of the Catholic Church §2277 calls euthanasia 'morally unacceptable'. The Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith's Declaration on Euthanasia (Iura et Bona, 1980) and Pope John Paul II's encyclical Evangelium Vitae (1995, §65) condemn it as 'a grave violation of the law of God'. BUT Catholic teaching does NOT demand life be prolonged at all costs - it distinguishes 'ordinary/proportionate' from 'extraordinary/disproportionate' means; refusing burdensome over-treatment ('therapeutic obstinacy') is legitimate, and palliative care and pain relief are positively encouraged.

whatToKnow.point: Protestant and Anglican positions vary - and Anglicans are NOT Protestant in the narrow sense examiners watch for.
whatToKnow.detail: The Anglican Communion (Church of England, Anglican Church of Australia) and most mainline churches officially oppose legalising euthanasia, emphasising compassion through palliative care over hastened death - but allow withdrawal of futile treatment. Some liberal Protestant ethicists (e.g. Joseph Fletcher, founder of situation ethics, 1966) argue the loving act may sometimes be to end suffering, prioritising agape over a fixed rule. The Uniting Church in Australia has generally allowed conscientious diversity rather than issuing one binding ruling, and some members and clergy have supported VAD with safeguards. Evangelical churches generally hold a firm sanctity-of-life line.

whatToKnow.point: Christians draw on more than proof-texts - natural law, agape ethics, and the example of Christ's suffering all feed the response.
whatToKnow.detail: Catholic reasoning rests on Thomas Aquinas's natural law (Summa Theologica) - life's self-preservation is a basic good, suicide contrary to nature, charity and the common good. The Christian theology of redemptive suffering (Christ on the cross; Romans 5:3–4, 'suffering produces endurance') frames suffering as bearable and meaningful, not purely an evil to be eliminated. Counter-voices use the same agape principle to argue compassion can justify mercy. The hospice movement (Dame Cicely Saunders, a devout Anglican, founded St Christopher's Hospice 1967) is the practical Christian alternative to euthanasia.

pointsOfView.label: Absolute prohibition (Magisterium)
pointsOfView.who: Roman Catholic Church
pointsOfView.summary: Euthanasia is intrinsically evil - a grave violation of God's law and the dignity of the person (Evangelium Vitae §65, Catechism §2277). Life is God's gift and only God is its author. BUT refusing disproportionate/extraordinary treatment is legitimate, and aggressive palliative care is a duty. The answer to suffering is care, not killing.

pointsOfView.label: Oppose legalisation, prioritise palliative care
pointsOfView.who: Anglican Church of Australia / Church of England and most mainline Protestant churches
pointsOfView.summary: Officially oppose legalising assisted dying, defending the vulnerable and the doctor-patient trust, while affirming that no one must be kept alive by futile, burdensome treatment. Compassion is expressed through hospice and palliative care, not hastened death. (Note: Anglicanism sits distinctly between Catholic and Protestant identity.)

pointsOfView.label: Situation-ethics / compassion case
pointsOfView.who: Joseph Fletcher and liberal Protestant ethicists
pointsOfView.summary: From situation ethics (1966): the only absolute is agape (self-giving love). No rule, including 'do not kill', is exceptionless; if the most loving action for a suffering, terminally ill person is to help them die with dignity, that can be the right Christian choice. A minority but examinable Christian voice.

pointsOfView.label: Pastoral diversity with safeguards
pointsOfView.who: Uniting Church in Australia (and individual believers)
pointsOfView.summary: Has generally allowed conscientious difference rather than issuing one binding ruling; some members and clergy have supported VAD legislation with strict safeguards as a compassionate response, while others uphold sanctity of life. There is no single binding Assembly ruling making VAD the church's position, so it is best stated as a pastoral stance, not a doctrine. Shows Christianity is not monolithic on this issue.
