B-ethics|Christianity.workedThesis: For the great majority of Christian traditions the sanctity of life - life as God's gift, made in his image - makes the deliberate ending of an innocent person's life morally impermissible; yet the most significant feature of the Christian response to euthanasia is not a blanket 'no' to all end-of-life choices but a carefully drawn line between killing and letting die, which allows the refusal of burdensome treatment and the embrace of palliative care while still condemning intentional killing.
paragraphPlan: Para 1 - Define the issue precisely (voluntary active euthanasia vs physician-assisted dying vs passive euthanasia) and locate it in the Australian context (Victoria's VAD Act 2017, with all six states now legislating VAD, while the territories - e.g. the ACT's 2024 law after the 2022 repeal of the federal Euthanasia Laws Act 1997 - sit outside the 'six states'), so the essay engages a real contemporary issue.

Para 2 - Establish the underpinning belief: sanctity of life from imago Dei (Genesis 1:27), reinforced by 1 Corinthians 6:19–20 and Job 1:21, plus the command against murder (Exodus 20:13, numbered the sixth commandment in Catholic/Lutheran tradition but the fifth in Reformed/Anglican numbering). Explain WHY this belief generates a presumption against euthanasia.

Para 3 - The Roman Catholic response in detail: Evangelium Vitae §65 and Catechism §2277 condemn euthanasia as intrinsically evil, BUT the ordinary/extraordinary means distinction and rejection of 'therapeutic obstinacy' mean refusing futile treatment and using strong pain relief (double effect) are permitted - evaluate how this nuance answers the 'cruel to prolong suffering' objection.

Para 4 - The diversity within Christianity: Anglican/mainline opposition-plus-palliative-care, set carefully apart from the Catholic absolutism and from Joseph Fletcher's situation ethics (1966) and Uniting Church conscientious diversity - evaluate which response is most coherent with core Christian commitments.

Para 5 - Evaluate significance: the practical Christian alternative (Cicely Saunders and the hospice movement, 1967) and the theology of redemptive suffering; judge how significant the sanctity-of-life belief is in shaping a distinctively Christian, compassion-grounded but life-affirming response.
B-ethics|Christianity.exemplarParagraph: The most significant feature of the Roman Catholic response to euthanasia is that it converts the abstract belief in the sanctity of life into a workable, and arguably humane, distinction rather than a demand to preserve biological life at all costs. Pope John Paul II's Evangelium Vitae (1995, §65) condemns euthanasia as 'a grave violation of the law of God', and the Catechism (§2277) calls the intentional ending of a suffering person's life 'morally unacceptable' - both flowing directly from the imago Dei of Genesis 1:27 and the conviction of Job 1:21 that it is the Lord who 'gave' and who 'has taken away'. Yet the same magisterial tradition insists, through the distinction between 'ordinary/proportionate' and 'extraordinary/disproportionate' means, that no Christian is obliged to submit to burdensome, futile over-treatment ('therapeutic obstinacy'), and that giving morphine to relieve pain - foreseeing but not intending death (the principle of double effect) - is positively good. This is what makes the position significant: it answers the most powerful objection to a sanctity-of-life ethic, namely that it is cruel to force a dying person to suffer, without conceding the core claim that intentionally killing the innocent breaches Exodus 20:13. The decisive moral line is therefore not between suffering and relief but between killing and letting die - and one can judge this distinction to be the strongest part of the Christian case, because it preserves both the inviolability of the person and a genuine compassion, locating the answer to suffering in palliative care (embodied in the devout Anglican Dame Cicely Saunders' founding of St Christopher's Hospice in 1967) rather than in a lethal act. The significance of the sanctity-of-life belief, then, lies less in producing a flat prohibition than in generating a whole alternative practice of dying well, which is precisely why the churches have so consistently opposed laws such as Victoria's Voluntary Assisted Dying Act 2017.

ladderForThis.C: Describe/explain the issue and belief: define euthanasia (voluntary active vs assisted dying vs passive) and explain the sanctity-of-life belief from Genesis 1:27 (imago Dei), the command against murder (Exodus 20:13 - the sixth commandment in Catholic/Lutheran, fifth in Reformed/Anglican/Jewish numbering) and Job 1:21, then state the main Christian response (e.g. Catholic opposition via Catechism §2277).
ladderForThis.B: Analyse: show HOW the belief produces the response and break down the internal distinctions - link imago Dei to the killing/letting-die line, the ordinary/extraordinary means distinction, and double effect; compare the Catholic, Anglican and situation-ethics positions and explain why they differ.
ladderForThis.A: Evaluate significance / critically analyse: judge how significant the sanctity-of-life belief is in shaping the response, weigh the compassion objection against the killing/letting-die distinction, assess which Christian position is most coherent, and reach a defended conclusion (e.g. that the belief generates not just a prohibition but an alternative practice of palliative care).

trapsForThisQuestion: Do NOT call Anglicans 'Protestant' without qualification - Anglicanism has a distinct identity (Catholic and Reformed heritage); examiners penalise the flat label. Treat the Anglican Communion as its own position.

Do NOT claim Christianity (or 'the Church') speaks with one voice - name the spread: Catholic absolutism, Anglican/mainline opposition-plus-palliative-care, Fletcher's situation ethics, Uniting Church conscientious diversity.

Do NOT conflate the types of euthanasia - voluntary active euthanasia, physician-assisted dying, and passive euthanasia are different; the killing/letting-die distinction is the whole hinge of the Christian argument and must be kept clear.

Avoid the error that the Catholic Church demands life be prolonged 'at all costs' - it explicitly permits refusing extraordinary/disproportionate means and rejects 'therapeutic obstinacy'. Stating the opposite is a factual error.
Do NOT just quote 'You shall not murder' and stop - at A-grade you must show the reasoning (imago Dei, double effect, natural law, redemptive suffering), not rely on a single proof-text.
Don't muddle the principle of double effect - pain relief that foreseeably shortens life is permitted; the intention must be relief, not death. Misstating intention vs foresight loses the nuance.

Keep the contemporary anchor accurate: Victoria's VAD Act was passed 2017 and became operative in 2019; it was the first Australian state scheme. Don't invent dates or attribute it to the wrong jurisdiction.

Don't use generic filler like 'Christians believe in the value of life' - always attach the named source (Genesis 1:27, Evangelium Vitae §65, Catechism §2277) and the specific variant holding the view.

B-ethics|Judaism.topic: Contemporary ethical issue in Judaism: euthanasia and end-of-life care (the goses, withdrawal of treatment, and the sanctity of life)

whatToKnow.point: The underpinning belief is the sanctity (pricelessness) of human life - pikuach nefesh - grounded in the Imago Dei and divine ownership of life
whatToKnow.detail: Genesis 1:27 ('God created humankind in his image') and Deuteronomy 32:39 ('I kill and I make alive') ground the idea that life belongs to God, not the individual. The Mishnah (Sanhedrin 4:5) teaches that to save one life is to save 'an entire world'. The principle of pikuach nefesh (saving a life) overrides almost all 613 commandments (mitzvot), including Shabbat - derived in the Talmud (Yoma 85b) from Leviticus 18:5, 'he shall live by them' (and not die by them). Life is of infinite value, so a dying person's remaining moments are worth as much as years.

whatToKnow.point: Active euthanasia is prohibited as murder; one may not hasten death even by a moment
whatToKnow.detail: The classic source is the Mishnah and Talmud's treatment of the dying person (the goses - a person actively dying, traditionally regarded as within about 72 hours of death). Tractate Semachot (Evel Rabbati 1:1-4) rules that a goses is 'a living person in all respects' and one who touches/moves them to hasten death is 'a shedder of blood'. Maimonides (Rambam, Mishneh Torah, Laws of Mourning 4:5, 12th c.) codifies this: closing the eyes of a dying person too soon is 'as if he sheds blood'. Joseph Karo's Shulchan Aruch (Yoreh De'ah 339:1, 16th c.) repeats it. So even a goses must not be actively killed - active euthanasia ('mercy killing') is assur (forbidden).

whatToKnow.point: BUT Jewish law distinguishes actively hastening death from removing an impediment to a natural death - the basis for some passive end-of-life decisions
whatToKnow.detail: Rabbi Moses Isserles (the Rema, 16th c.), in his gloss on Shulchan Aruch (Yoreh De'ah 339:1), rules that while one may not actively hasten death, one MAY remove an external impediment that is artificially prolonging the dying process - his classic examples are removing a clattering noise (e.g. salt on the tongue, a woodchopper near the house) that keeps the soul from departing. Modern Orthodox poskim (decisors) like Rabbi Moshe Feinstein (Igrot Moshe) and Rabbi J. David Bleich debate whether a ventilator or chemotherapy counts as such an 'impediment'. This is the bridge to permitting withdrawal/withholding in narrow cases - NOT a blanket permission.

whatToKnow.point: The three major movements diverge sharply on how far passive measures, patient autonomy and even active aid-in-dying can go
whatToKnow.detail: ORTHODOX (e.g. Rabbi J. David Bleich, Rabbi Moshe Feinstein): strictest - life is not the patient's to relinquish; generally oppose withdrawing a running ventilator but may permit withholding new aggressive treatment and pain relief. Orthodox opinion is itself a spectrum: Bleich sits at the stricter end, while some poskim more readily permit WITHHOLDING (rather than withdrawing) burdensome treatment. CONSERVATIVE/Masorti: the Rabbinical Assembly's Committee on Jewish Law and Standards (CJLS) has adopted competing responsa over time. Rabbi Elliot Dorff's earlier responsum ('Matters of Life and Death', 1990s) permits withholding/withdrawing life-support and even artificial nutrition for a terminal patient while still forbidding active killing/assisted suicide; Rabbi Avram Reisner takes a more restrictive line on withdrawing nutrition. Crucially, in 2020 Dorff authored a further CJLS-recognised responsum ('Assisted Suicide/Aid in Dying Reconsidered') that reverses his earlier stance and PERMITS physician aid-in-dying in narrow cases where it is legal - so the Conservative movement now holds competing responsa on active aid-in-dying itself, not merely on passive withdrawal. REFORM (CCAR): emphasises autonomy and compassion most strongly, accepts advance directives and withdrawal of futile treatment, but the Central Conference of American Rabbis still formally rejects active euthanasia/assisted suicide as incompatible with sanctity of life.

whatToKnow.point: Pain relief and the 'double effect' are permitted; suffering does not override the prohibition on killing
whatToKnow.detail: Across movements, giving high-dose analgesia (e.g. morphine) to relieve a dying patient's pain is permitted even if it foreseeably shortens life, PROVIDED the intent is pain relief, not death - a Jewish parallel to the principle of double effect. The dignity of the dying (kevod ha-met / kevod ha-briyot) and refraining from causeless suffering matter, but Judaism does not treat 'quality of life' as a licence to end it. This distinguishes the traditional Jewish view from secular autonomy-based pro-euthanasia arguments (e.g. Peter Singer).

pointsOfView.label: Orthodox - strict sanctity, minimal passive allowance (itself a spectrum)
pointsOfView.who: Rabbi J. David Bleich; Rabbi Moshe Feinstein (Igrot Moshe)
pointsOfView.summary: Life belongs to God and is of infinite value, so it is never the patient's to surrender. Active euthanasia is murder (per Maimonides/Shulchan Aruch). Bleich sits at the stricter end and generally opposes withdrawing an active life-support measure such as a running ventilator, though Feinstein allows withholding new burdensome treatment from a clearly terminal patient and permits pain relief; some Orthodox poskim more readily permit withholding (not withdrawing) burdensome care. Autonomy does not trump the divine ownership of life.

pointsOfView.label: Conservative/Masorti - competing CJLS responsa; position evolving on active aid-in-dying
pointsOfView.who: Rabbi Elliot Dorff (Rabbinical Assembly CJLS responsa, 1990s and 2020)
pointsOfView.summary: Working from the Rema's 'remove the impediment' principle, Dorff's earlier responsum permits a terminally ill patient to refuse or withdraw life-support, ventilators, and even artificial nutrition/hydration when treatment only prolongs dying, while still forbidding active killing/assisted suicide. A competing CJLS responsum by Rabbi Avram Reisner is more cautious about withdrawing nutrition. However, Dorff's 2020 CJLS-recognised responsum reverses his earlier line and permits physician aid-in-dying in narrow cases where it is legal - so the movement now holds competing responsa on active aid-in-dying itself, making Conservative Judaism a live example of a position shifting over time.

pointsOfView.label: Reform - autonomy and compassion foregrounded
pointsOfView.who: Central Conference of American Rabbis (CCAR)
pointsOfView.summary: Reform Judaism places the strongest weight on patient autonomy, dignity, and the duty to relieve suffering; it endorses advance directives and withdrawing futile treatment. Yet the CCAR still formally rejects active euthanasia and physician-assisted suicide as inconsistent with the sanctity of life and the tradition's reverence for the Imago Dei.

pointsOfView.label: Secular contrast (NOT Jewish) - used as a foil
pointsOfView.who: Peter Singer / autonomy-based bioethics
pointsOfView.summary: A purely autonomy- and quality-of-life-based ethic would permit active voluntary euthanasia. Naming this as the contrast clarifies WHY most of the Jewish tradition is distinctive: it subordinates autonomy to the divine ownership of life, so even where Jewish authorities accept pain relief and passive measures (and, in the Conservative case, limited aid-in-dying) they reason from sanctity-of-life precedent rather than from autonomy alone.
