Dorff, 'Matters of Life and Death: A Jewish Approach to Modern Medical Ethics' (1998), with his 1990s CJLS responsum (the earlier withdrawal position) and his later 2020 CJLS responsum 'Assisted Suicide/Aid in Dying Reconsidered' (permitting aid-in-dying in narrow legal cases)
sources.useFor: Articulating the Conservative position on withdrawing/withholding life-support and the movement's later shift on active aid-in-dying
sources.snippet: futile prolongation is not mandated; the later responsum reconsiders aid-in-dying

sources.reference: J. David Bleich, 'Bioethical Dilemmas: A Jewish Perspective'
sources.useFor: The strict Orthodox position against withdrawing active life-support
sources.snippet: life is not the patient's to relinquish

B-ethics|Judaism.workedThesis: While all three major movements of Judaism root their response to euthanasia in the sanctity of life (pikuach nefesh) and so reject active 'mercy killing' as bloodshed in their classical reasoning, the tradition's significance lies in how the Rema's active-versus-passive distinction allows Orthodox, Conservative and Reform authorities to diverge meaningfully on withdrawing treatment - and how the Conservative movement's own shift from forbidding to permitting limited aid-in-dying (Dorff's 1990s versus 2020 CJLS responsa) reveals that the Jewish answer is not a single fixed rule but a graded, evolving, source-driven negotiation between the infinite value of life and the duty to relieve suffering.
paragraphPlan: Paragraph 1 - Establish the underpinning belief: sanctity of life as divine ownership and Imago Dei (Gen 1:27, Deut 32:39, Mishnah Sanhedrin 4:5, pikuach nefesh from Yoma 85b). State WHY life is of infinite value.

Paragraph 2 - Explain the named guidance on active euthanasia: the goses in Tractate Semachot, codified by Maimonides (Mishneh Torah, Laws of Mourning 4:5) and the Shulchan Aruch (YD 339:1) - active hastening = bloodshed. This is the shared classical baseline.

Paragraph 3 - Analyse the pivotal distinction: the Rema's gloss permitting removal of an impediment to natural death; how this opens space for passive measures and how poskim (Feinstein, Bleich) debate whether a ventilator qualifies; note Orthodox opinion is itself a spectrum (withholding vs withdrawing).

Paragraph 4 - Analyse denominational divergence: Orthodox (Bleich/Feinstein) vs Conservative (Dorff's earlier withdrawal responsum and the Reisner counter-responsum, then Dorff's 2020 aid-in-dying responsum) vs Reform (CCAR), plus the shared acceptance of double-effect pain relief.

Paragraph 5 (EVALUATE) - Assess the SIGNIFICANCE: why the tradition's graded, evolving response matters today (ventilators, advance directives, the Conservative shift), how it differs from secular autonomy ethics (Singer foil), and judge whether the active/passive line is coherent or strained by modern technology.
B-ethics|Judaism.exemplarParagraph: The significance of Judaism's response to euthanasia lies not in a single fixed prohibition but in how the Rema's active-versus-passive distinction allows an ancient sanctity-of-life ethic to remain workable - and even to evolve - under modern medical technology, and it is precisely here that the tradition's authority is both most useful and most contested. Because life is understood as God's possession rather than the patient's own (Deuteronomy 32:39; Mishnah Sanhedrin 4:5), the classical reasoning of every movement resists active 'mercy killing', which is why autonomy-centred Reform Judaism, through the CCAR, still stops short of the secular conclusion reached by autonomy-based ethicists such as Peter Singer. Yet the very same sources generate real divergence: the Rema's sixteenth-century image of removing a 'clattering noise' that delays the soul's departure (Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh De'ah 339:1) was never written with ventilators in mind, so when Rabbi Elliot Dorff permits a terminal patient to have life-support withdrawn while Rabbi J. David Bleich resists removing a running ventilator, both are arguably faithful readings of the same impediment principle. The clearest proof that the Jewish answer is a living negotiation rather than a frozen rule is the Conservative movement's own shift: Dorff's earlier responsum forbade assisted suicide, yet his 2020 CJLS-recognised responsum reconsidered the question and permitted physician aid-in-dying in narrow cases where it is legal, so that even the once-confident claim that 'all movements reject active euthanasia' now holds securely only for Orthodox and Reform Judaism. This demonstrates that the most significant feature of the Jewish position is its graded, casuistic method - ruling case-by-case from precedent rather than by abstract principle - which is what lets one tradition hold the infinite value of life and the duty to relieve suffering in tension at once, and even revise its conclusions as conditions change. The enduring difficulty, however, is that the binary of 'hastening' versus 'merely removing an impediment' was forged in a world without machines that breathe for a patient, so the distinction that gives the tradition its flexibility is also the point at which its coherence is most open to challenge - making the issue a genuine test of whether classical halachic reasoning can govern contemporary intensive care.

ladderForThis.C: DESCRIBE/EXPLAIN: State that Judaism holds the sanctity of life (pikuach nefesh) and explain that active euthanasia is classically forbidden as bloodshed, citing the goses, Maimonides and the Shulchan Aruch, while pain relief and some passive measures are permitted.
ladderForThis.B: ANALYSE: Examine HOW the Rema's active/passive distinction works and WHY Orthodox, Conservative and Reform authorities reach different conclusions on withdrawing treatment - and, for Conservative Judaism, on active aid-in-dying - from the same shared sources, showing cause and effect between text and ruling.
ladderForThis.A: EVALUATE: Judge the SIGNIFICANCE and coherence of the Jewish response - assess whether the casuistic 'remove the impediment' principle still functions under modern technology, use the Conservative movement's shift (Dorff 1990s vs 2020) as evidence that the tradition's method is graded and evolving, contrast it with secular autonomy ethics to show what is distinctive, and weigh how successfully the tradition balances infinite life-value against suffering.

trapsForThisQuestion: DO NOT claim a blanket cross-movement rejection of ACTIVE euthanasia. Orthodox and Reform (CCAR) do reject active euthanasia/assisted suicide, but Conservative Judaism is now split/evolving: Dorff's 2020 CJLS responsum permits physician aid-in-dying in narrow legal cases. State the rejection as classical/baseline and note the Conservative shift.
DO NOT treat the three movements as having one uniform view - name Orthodox, Conservative/Masorti and Reform and show they DIFFER on passive measures (and, for Conservative, on active aid-in-dying).
DO NOT use Christian vocabulary - write 'rabbi', 'synagogue', 'halacha', 'Tanakh/Hebrew Bible', NOT 'priest', 'church', 'chapel', or 'Old Testament'.
DO NOT confuse 'sanctity of life' with 'quality of life' - the traditional Jewish reasoning rejects quality-of-life as a licence to end life; that is the secular/autonomy view.
DO NOT invent a single specific verse 'commandment against euthanasia' - there is no one verse; the ruling is built from the goses sources, Maimonides and the Shulchan Aruch via rabbinic reasoning.
DO NOT call the goses period vague - it refers to a person actively dying (traditionally within about 72 hours of death); use the term correctly.
DO NOT claim pain relief is forbidden - double-effect analgesia is broadly accepted; the forbidden act is INTENDING death.
DO NOT attribute the impediment-removal allowance to the Talmud directly - it is the Rema (Moshe Isserles), a 16th-century gloss on the Shulchan Aruch.
DO NOT treat 'Orthodox' as monolithic - Bleich is at the stricter end; some Orthodox poskim more readily permit WITHHOLDING (not withdrawing) burdensome treatment.

C-women|Christianity.topic: Change OR lack of change in Christian teaching on the place of women: the named points of view within the tradition, who holds them, and the primary + secondary sources behind each

whatToKnow.point: The 'foundational tension' in the New Testament: the SAME text is read as both restrictive and liberating, and this is the engine of every later debate.
whatToKnow.detail: Restrictive (the 'household codes' / Haustafeln): 1 Corinthians 14:34 ('women should remain silent in the churches'); 1 Timothy 2:11-12 ('I do not permit a woman to teach or to assume authority over a man; she must be quiet'); Ephesians 5:22-24 ('Wives, submit yourselves to your own husbands'). Egalitarian: Galatians 3:28 ('there is neither... male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus'); the women at the resurrection (Mary Magdalene as the first witness, John 20:11-18 - later called 'apostle to the apostles'); Phoebe named a 'deacon' (diakonos) and Junia, in Romans 16:1,7, described with the contested phrase episemoi en tois apostolois - read either as 'outstanding among the apostles' (so herself an apostle) or 'well known to the apostles'. The exam reward is showing you know the SAME tradition holds both, and that interpretation - not the text alone - drives change.

whatToKnow.point: The historic position was patriarchal and remarkably stable for ~1900 years, anchored by named theologians - so 'lack of change' is the genuinely strong default the essay must reckon with.
whatToKnow.detail: Tertullian (c.160-225) called woman 'the devil's gateway'. Augustine of Hippo (354-430) held that woman images God only when joined to her husband (De Trinitate). Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274), drawing on Aristotle, described woman, quoting Aristotle, as 'deficient and misbegotten' (deficiens et occasionatus, Summa Theologiae I q.92 a.1) - yet immediately qualified that woman is NOT misbegotten as regards human nature in general, and is equal in soul. This consensus held that women could not be ordained or teach authoritatively, and it was NOT seriously institutionally challenged until the 19th-20th centuries. A good essay treats continuity as the baseline and change as the thing requiring explanation.

whatToKnow.point: Ordination of women is the single sharpest dividing line TODAY and splits the tradition cleanly - name who ordains and who does not.
whatToKnow.detail: DO ordain women as priests/ministers: most Anglican provinces (Church of England ordained women priests 1994 after the 1992 General Synod vote; women bishops since 2014, Libby Lane first), the Uniting Church in Australia (from its 1977 formation), Lutheran churches, the Methodist Church, the Salvation Army (Catherine Booth defended women's right to preach in her 1859 pamphlet Female Ministry; the Salvation Army, named 1878, commissioned women officers from its origins), most Presbyterian churches. DO NOT ordain women: the Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church. Pope John Paul II's Ordinatio Sacerdotalis (1994) declared the Church 'has no authority whatsoever' to ordain women and that this judgement is 'definitively held' - reaffirmed by the CDF in 1995. Reason given: the priest acts in persona Christi and the all-male apostles. This is the cleanest 'change vs no change' contrast available.

whatToKnow.point: The Catholic 'no change in ordination' coexists with REAL development of teaching - this nuance separates A from B.
whatToKnow.detail: Pope John Paul II's apostolic letter Mulieris Dignitatem (1988) affirmed the 'equal dignity' of women and a 'new feminism'; his 1995 'Letter to Women' apologised for historic wrongs. Vatican II's Gaudium et Spes (1965) condemned discrimination based on sex. Pope Francis appointed women to senior Vatican roles (e.g. Sr Raffaella Petrini as head of the Governorate of Vatican City State - formally President of the Pontifical Commission and President of the Governorate of Vatican City State, effective 1 March 2025) and opened the question of women DEACONS via two study commissions (2016, 2020), though without changing doctrine. So Catholicism shows doctrinal CONTINUITY on priesthood but genuine DEVELOPMENT on dignity and lay/administrative roles. Avoid the trap of saying 'the Catholic Church has not changed at all'.

whatToKnow.point: Christian feminist theology is a named scholarly movement (secondary voices) that reinterprets the primary texts - cite specific scholars, not 'feminists say'.
whatToKnow.detail: Rosemary Radford Ruether, Sexism and God-Talk (1983), argued patriarchy distorts the tradition and asked whether a male saviour can save women, answering that Jesus' praxis was liberating. Elisabeth Schussler Fiorenza, In Memory of Her (1983), reconstructed a 'discipleship of equals' in earliest Christianity and pioneered a 'hermeneutics of suspicion'. Phyllis Trible, Texts of Terror (1984), re-read violent OT narratives about women. On the other side, evangelical complementarians John Piper and Wayne Grudem edited Recovering Biblical Manhood and Womanhood (1991) and founded the Council on Biblical Manhood and Womanhood (CBMW) defending distinct roles; the rival evangelical group Christians for Biblical Equality (CBE) argues for full egalitarian ministry. Naming both sides shows the debate runs WITHIN as well as across denominations.

whatToKnow.point: Concrete recent dates give the essay 'currency' and let you argue change is accelerating - examiners reward specific contemporary evidence.
whatToKnow.detail: Church of England women priests 1994; first woman bishop Libby Lane consecrated 26 Jan 2015. The Episcopal Church (USA) elected Katharine Jefferts Schori as Presiding Bishop 2006. Pope Francis gave women the vote at the 2023-2024 Synod on Synodality for the first time. The Southern Baptist Convention moved the OTHER way, expelling churches with women pastors (e.g. Saddleback Church, 2023) - a clear case of REASSERTED continuity/backlash. Using both a 'change' and a 'counter-change' from the same decade is exactly how to evaluate significance rather than just narrate.

pointsOfView.label: Roman Catholic magisterial position
pointsOfView.who: The Roman Catholic Church (Magisterium; John Paul II, CDF, continued under Benedict XVI and Francis)
pointsOfView.summary: Equal dignity of women is affirmed and developed (Mulieris Dignitatem 1988; Letter to Women 1995), but the male-only priesthood is unchangeable doctrine (Ordinatio Sacerdotalis 1994) because the apostles were men and the priest acts in persona Christi. Real change in roles and dignity; deliberate NON-change in ordination. The diaconate question is open but unresolved.
pointsOfView.source: Ordinatio Sacerdotalis (1994); Mulieris Dignitatem (1988); Gaudium et Spes (1965)

pointsOfView.label: Eastern Orthodox position
pointsOfView.who: The Eastern Orthodox Church (e.g. Ecumenical Patriarchate; theologians like Kallistos Ware)
pointsOfView.summary: Maintains an all-male priesthood grounded in Holy Tradition and the iconic role of the priest, with strong veneration of the Theotokos (Mary) and female saints; has discussed reviving the ancient order of deaconesses (notably the 2016 decision of the Patriarchate of Alexandria), but this is a revival of the DIACONATE, which Orthodox theology treats as a distinct order, explicitly NOT a step toward the priesthood, which remains closed. Continuity is framed as fidelity to Tradition, not mere conservatism.
pointsOfView.source: Holy Tradition; veneration of the Theotokos; 2016 Alexandria deaconess decision

pointsOfView.label: Anglican / mainline Protestant egalitarian position
pointsOfView.who: Most of the Anglican Communion, the Uniting Church in Australia, Methodists, Lutherans, Presbyterians
pointsOfView.summary: Reads Galatians 3:28 and the prominence of women in the Gospels as the controlling principle and treats 1 Timothy 2:12 as culturally conditioned; has changed practice to ordain women as priests/ministers and bishops. Treated as faithful development, the Spirit leading the Church into fuller truth (John 16:13).
pointsOfView.source: Church of England General Synod 1992; Uniting Church Basis of Union 1977; Galatians 3:28

pointsOfView.label: Evangelical complementarian position
pointsOfView.who: John Piper, Wayne Grudem, the Council on Biblical Manhood and Womanhood (CBMW); Southern Baptist Convention
pointsOfView.summary: Men and women are equal in worth but created for distinct, complementary roles; teaching/governing church office and headship in marriage are reserved to men, citing 1 Timothy 2:12 and Ephesians 5:23. Defends LACK of change as biblical fidelity, and has actively reasserted it (Saddleback expulsion 2023).
pointsOfView.source: Recovering Biblical Manhood and Womanhood (Piper & Grudem, 1991); Danvers Statement 1987; 1 Tim 2:12

pointsOfView.label: Christian feminist / egalitarian-evangelical scholarship
pointsOfView.who: Rosemary Radford Ruether, Elisabeth Schussler Fiorenza, Phyllis Trible; Christians for Biblical Equality (CBE)
pointsOfView.summary: Argues the tradition is genuinely patriarchal but recoverable: uses a hermeneutics of suspicion to expose how restrictive texts were privileged, reconstructs an early 'discipleship of equals', and grounds full equality in Jesus' own practice. Demands and explains change as a return to the Gospel's liberating core.
pointsOfView.source: Sexism and God-Talk (Ruether 1983); In Memory of Her (Schussler Fiorenza 1983); Texts of Terror (Trible 1984)

sources.reference: Galatians 3:28
sources.useFor: The egalitarian anchor text; quoted by every change-supporting tradition ('neither... male nor female... one in Christ Jesus').
sources.snippet: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek... there is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.'

sources.reference: 1 Timothy 2:11-12
sources.useFor: The restrictive anchor; cited by Catholics, Orthodox and complementarians for an all-male teaching office.
sources.snippet: 'I do not permit a woman to teach or to assume authority over a man; she must be quiet.'

sources.reference: Romans 16:1,7
sources.useFor: Evidence of women's early leadership (Phoebe a deacon/servant; Junia either 'outstanding among' or 'well known to' the apostles) used by egalitarians - the disputed translation is itself part of the argument.
sources.snippet: Paul commends 'Phoebe, a deacon (diakonos - also rendered 'servant') of the church' and greets 'Junia', described as either 'outstanding among the apostles' or 'well known to the apostles' - the translation itself is contested and is part of the debate.

sources.reference: Ordinatio Sacerdotalis (John Paul II, 1994)
sources.useFor: The definitive Catholic statement of NON-change on priesthood; the 'lack of change' centrepiece.
sources.snippet: The Church 'has no authority whatsoever to confer priestly ordination on women' and this is to be 'definitively held'.

sources.reference: Mulieris Dignitatem (John Paul II, 1988)
sources.useFor: Shows Catholic DEVELOPMENT on dignity coexisting with continuity on priesthood - the key nuance.
sources.snippet: Affirms the 'equal dignity' of women and calls for a 'new feminism'.

sources.reference: Elisabeth Schussler Fiorenza, In Memory of Her (1983)
sources.useFor: Naming the feminist reconstruction of early Christianity and the 'hermeneutics of suspicion'.
sources.snippet: Reconstructs earliest Christianity as a 'discipleship of equals' later obscured by patriarchy.

sources.reference: Rosemary Radford Ruether, Sexism and God-Talk (1983)
sources.useFor: The classic Christian feminist evaluation of whether the tradition can be redeemed from sexism.
sources.snippet: Argues patriarchy distorts but does not define the tradition; Jesus' praxis is liberating for women.

sources.reference: John Piper & Wayne Grudem (eds), Recovering Biblical Manhood and Womanhood (1991)
sources.useFor: The articulate scholarly DEFENCE of lack of change (complementarianism) - needed for balance.
sources.snippet: Men and women are equal in worth but called to distinct, complementary roles in church and home.

C-women|Christianity.workedThesis: While the New Testament itself holds restrictive 'household code' texts (1 Timothy 2:12) alongside egalitarian ones (Galatians 3:28), the most significant development in Christian teaching on women is not a single tradition-wide shift but a DIVERGENCE: Anglican and mainline churches have changed practice decisively (women priests 1994, bishops 2015) while the Roman Catholic and Orthodox Churches have deliberately preserved continuity on ordination even as they develop their teaching on women's dignity - meaning the place of women has become the clearest fault-line of disagreement WITHIN Christianity rather than a settled matter of either change or stasis.
paragraphPlan: INTRO: establish the NT 'foundational tension' (Gal 3:28 vs 1 Tim 2:12) and state the thesis - the real story is divergence, not uniform change or stasis.

BODY 1 (describe/explain, C): the historic patriarchal baseline and its named theologians (Tertullian, Augustine, Aquinas) - establish ~1900 years of continuity so change becomes the thing to be explained.

BODY 2 (analyse, B): the Anglican/mainline CHANGE - how Gal 3:28 and the Gospel women became the controlling lens; concrete dates (CofE 1994, Libby Lane 2015, Uniting Church 1977); change framed as faithful development.

BODY 3 (analyse, B): the Catholic/Orthodox LACK of change on ordination - Ordinatio Sacerdotalis, in persona Christi, Holy Tradition - BUT paired with genuine development of dignity (Mulieris Dignitatem, women in Vatican roles, the deaconess question).

BODY 4 (evaluate significance, A): weigh it - the feminist scholars (Ruether, Schussler Fiorenza) vs complementarians (Piper, Grudem) show the dispute is hermeneutical; use the Saddleback 2023 counter-change to argue significance lies in the FAULT-LINE itself.
CONCLUSION: synthesise - the most significant change is that 'the place of women' has moved from settled assumption to the defining internal disagreement of modern Christianity.
C-women|Christianity.exemplarParagraph: The significance of these developments is best judged not by counting which churches changed, but by recognising what the disagreement reveals about how Christianity authorises change at all - and on this measure the shift is profound. The Anglican decision to ordain women (England, 1994; first woman bishop Libby Lane, 2015) and the Catholic refusal in Ordinatio Sacerdotalis (1994) both appeal to the SAME New Testament, which is precisely why the divide is so significant: it exposes that the 'place of women' was never settled by the text alone but by which texts a tradition privileges and how it reads them. Elisabeth Schussler Fiorenza's hermeneutics of suspicion (In Memory of Her, 1983) and Rosemary Radford Ruether's claim that Jesus' praxis was liberating reframe change as a RETURN to a buried 'discipleship of equals', whereas John Piper and Wayne Grudem's complementarianism reframes continuity as fidelity, not prejudice - so the debate is not progress versus backwardness but a genuine contest over biblical authority. That this contest remains live, and is even hardening, is shown by the Southern Baptist Convention's 2023 expulsion of Saddleback Church for appointing women pastors: a deliberate RE-assertion of non-change in the very decade Pope Francis gave women a vote at the Synod. The most significant feature, therefore, is not that Christianity has uniformly changed or uniformly held firm, but that the place of women has become the sharpest internal fault-line of the modern church - a question on which the tradition now visibly disagrees with itself, making it a test case for whether Christianity understands fidelity as preservation or as development.

ladderForThis.C: DESCRIBE/EXPLAIN: Set out the NT tension (Galatians 3:28 vs 1 Timothy 2:12) and the historic patriarchal position with its named theologians (Tertullian, Augustine, Aquinas). State accurately WHICH churches ordain women (Anglican, Uniting, Methodist, Lutheran) and which do not (Roman Catholic, Eastern Orthodox), with at least one correct date (e.g. CofE women priests 1994).
ladderForThis.B: ANALYSE: Explain WHY the divergence happens - that each side reads the same scripture differently (controlling-text choice: Gal 3:28 as principle vs 1 Tim 2:12 as command), and link positions to their reasons (in persona Christi and Holy Tradition for non-change; faithful development and Gospel women for change). Show change and continuity are each ARGUED, not accidental.
ladderForThis.A: EVALUATE SIGNIFICANCE: Judge what the change/non-change means for the tradition - argue (with a thesis) that the deepest significance is the hermeneutical fault-line itself; weigh feminist scholars (Ruether, Schussler Fiorenza) against complementarians (Piper, Grudem); use a counter-example (Saddleback 2023) to show change is contested and even reversing; conclude on whether fidelity means preservation or development.

trapsForThisQuestion: Calling Anglicans 'Protestant' without care: in Tasmanian SOR, Anglicanism is usually treated as a distinct 'middle way' (via media) - say 'Anglican' specifically rather than lumping it under Protestant. Likewise never imply Catholics or Orthodox are Protestant.

Claiming 'the Catholic Church has not changed at all' - this is wrong and loses marks. It has NOT changed on priesthood but HAS developed its teaching on dignity (Mulieris Dignitatem 1988, women in Vatican leadership, the open deaconess question). State both.

Saying 'all Orthodox / all Anglicans' do one thing. The Anglican Communion is internally split (some provinces, e.g. parts of Africa, and Anglo-Catholic groups, reject women priests); avoid sweeping universals.

Treating the restrictive texts as obviously 'wrong' or the change as simply 'progress'. The complementarian view (Piper, Grudem, SBC) is a serious, current position held by millions - present it as a reasoned stance, not bigotry, to show evaluation rather than bias.
Confusing the Virgin Mary's high honour with women's authority - Marian devotion (Theotokos) coexists with an all-male priesthood; do not present veneration of Mary as evidence women could lead.

Vague filler like 'over time attitudes changed' with no dates. Anchor every claim: 1994 (CofE priests / Ordinatio Sacerdotalis - note the SAME year cuts both ways), 2015 (Libby Lane), 1977 (Uniting Church), 2023 (Saddleback / Synod vote).

Misattributing scholars: In Memory of Her is Schussler Fiorenza (not Ruether); Sexism and God-Talk is Ruether; Texts of Terror is Trible. Get the author–title pairing exactly right or do not name the book.

C-women|Judaism.topic: The place of women in Judaism: change and continuity in the tradition's teaching, the named variants and figures who hold differing positions, and the primary and secondary sources that ground them.

whatToKnow.point: The traditional/halakhic baseline: women are exempt from time-bound positive commandments and excluded from forming a minyan and from public Torah reading, which historically barred them from public ritual leadership.
whatToKnow.detail: Mishnah Kiddushin 1:7 establishes that women are exempt from positive commandments that are time-bound (mitzvot aseh shehazman gerama). The minyan (quorum of 10) is derived in the Talmud (Megillah 23b) and counts only adult males in Orthodox practice. This is the textual root of the 'separate roles' position, NOT a claim that women are spiritually inferior. The traditional defence (e.g. by figures like Rabbi Samson Raphael Hirsch, 1808-1888) is that women are exempt because they are already more spiritually attuned and tied to home-based mitzvot, not because they are lesser.

whatToKnow.point: Women's distinctive positive obligations and honoured biblical/rabbinic figures show the tradition never regarded women as religiously marginal.
whatToKnow.detail: The three 'women's mitzvot' are niddah (menstrual purity laws), challah (separating dough), and hadlakat nerot (lighting Shabbat candles) - remembered by the mnemonic ChaNaH (Hannah), the initials of Challah, Niddah and Hadlakat nerot. Biblical women of stature include the matriarchs Sarah, Rebecca, Rachel and Leah; the prophet Miriam (Exodus 15:20, 'Miriam the prophetess'); Deborah, a judge and prophet who led Israel (Judges 4-5); Huldah the prophet (2 Kings 22). The Talmud names Bruriah (2nd century CE), wife of Rabbi Meir, as a woman of exceptional learning. These ground the argument that 'separate' was historically framed as honoured, not subordinate.

whatToKnow.point: The agunah problem is the sharpest live example of LACK of change: Jewish divorce law still structurally disadvantages women.
whatToKnow.detail: Under halakha a divorce (get) must be given by the husband to the wife of his free will (rooted in Deuteronomy 24:1). A woman whose husband refuses, disappears, or cannot consent becomes an agunah ('chained woman'), unable to remarry. Orthodox authorities have NOT changed the core rule; instead they use workarounds (the prenuptial agreement promoted by the Beth Din of America since the 1990s; the RCA halachic prenup). The Conservative movement created the Lieberman clause (1953) and uses the takkanah/hafka'at kiddushin mechanism; Reform Judaism abandoned the get requirement for civil divorce. This is the key 'continuity vs change differs by denomination' case study.

whatToKnow.point: Reform Judaism led the most radical CHANGE, from the 19th century through to female ordination.
whatToKnow.detail: The 1846 Breslau Conference of Reform rabbis (Germany) resolved in principle for the religious equality of women. Reform abolished the mechitza (separation barrier) and counted women in worship. Regina Jonas was ordained in Berlin in 1935 - the first female rabbi in history (murdered at Auschwitz, 1944). In the USA, Sally Priesand became the first American female rabbi, ordained 1972 at Hebrew Union College (Reform). Reform fully ordains women and LGBTQ+ clergy and treats gender equality as a core principle of Progressive Judaism.

whatToKnow.point: Conservative (Masorti) Judaism changed WITHIN the halakhic framework, debating each step through its law committee - making it the key 'change but with continuity' case.
whatToKnow.detail: The Conservative movement's Committee on Jewish Law and Standards (CJLS) is the mechanism. In 1973 the CJLS permitted counting women in the minyan. In 1983 the Jewish Theological Seminary (JTS) voted to ordain women; Amy Eilberg became the first Conservative female rabbi in 1985. This contrasts with Reform (change by abandoning halakhic obligation) and Orthodoxy (no ordination) - Conservative argues halakha itself, properly interpreted, permits the change.

whatToKnow.point: Orthodoxy itself is NOT monolithic: Modern Orthodoxy has cautiously expanded women's roles while Haredi (ultra-Orthodox) Judaism maintains strict separation - and female ordination remains the contested frontier.
whatToKnow.detail: Modern Orthodox developments include Yoatzot Halacha (women advisers on niddah law, trained at Nishmat from 1997), and the controversial title for women clergy: Sara Hurwitz was titled 'Maharat' by Rabbi Avi Weiss in 2009, a title he upgraded to 'Rabba' in 2010. The Orthodox Union and the Agudath Israel (Haredi) reject female ordination; the RCA passed resolutions against it (2010, 2015). Partnership minyanim (e.g. Shira Hadasha, Jerusalem, founded 2002) let women lead permitted parts within an Orthodox framework. Haredi communities retain the mechitza and exclude women from public ritual leadership entirely.

whatToKnow.point: Activist movements and a key court ruling mark the politics of change, especially in Israel.
whatToKnow.detail: Women of the Wall (Nashot HaKotel), founded 1988, campaign for women's right to pray aloud, read Torah and wear tallit at the Western Wall (Kotel). The issue is entangled with the Chief Rabbinate's control of the site. Internationally, Blu Greenberg (Orthodox feminist, founder of JOFA, the Jewish Orthodox Feminist Alliance, 1997) coined the much-quoted line that 'where there is a rabbinic will, there is a halakhic way' - the slogan of those arguing Orthodoxy CAN change from within.

pointsOfView.label: Reform / Progressive - full egalitarian change
pointsOfView.who: Reform Judaism; figures: Regina Jonas (1935), Sally Priesand (1972); rooted in 1846 Breslau Conference
pointsOfView.summary: Gender equality is a moral principle that overrides time-bound halakhic distinctions. Women count fully, lead worship, and are ordained as rabbis and cantors. The tradition SHOULD change because ethical monotheism evolves; older gender rules reflect their historical context, not eternal divine will.

pointsOfView.label: Conservative / Masorti - change through halakha
pointsOfView.who: Conservative Judaism via the CJLS and JTS; figure: Amy Eilberg (first Conservative woman rabbi, 1985)
pointsOfView.summary: Halakha is binding but developmental. Through proper legal reasoning the law committee can and did permit counting women in a minyan (1973) and ordaining women (1983/85). Change is legitimate ONLY when argued from within the halakhic system - distinguishing them sharply from Reform.

pointsOfView.label: Modern Orthodox - cautious expansion, no ordination consensus
pointsOfView.who: Modern Orthodoxy; figures: Blu Greenberg (JOFA), Sara Hurwitz (Maharat 2009 / Rabba 2010), Rabbi Avi Weiss; institutions Nishmat, Yeshivat Maharat
pointsOfView.summary: Women's learning and leadership can expand within halakha (Yoatzot Halacha, partnership minyanim, advanced Talmud study for women), and some ordain women under new titles. But mainstream Orthodox bodies (RCA, OU) reject female rabbinic ordination - so this camp is internally split between expansionists and traditionalists.

pointsOfView.label: Haredi (ultra-Orthodox) - continuity, separate roles
pointsOfView.who: Haredi Judaism; bodies like Agudath Israel; intellectual root in R. Samson Raphael Hirsch's 'separate but honoured' framing
pointsOfView.summary: Women's role is divinely ordained, centred on home, family and the women's mitzvot (niddah, challah, candle-lighting). The mechitza and exclusion from minyan/public leadership are non-negotiable. 'Equality' as the secular world frames it misreads a system in which the roles differ but are equally valued; the tradition does NOT change.

sources.reference: Mishnah Kiddushin 1:7
sources.useFor: Establishing the halakhic baseline - women exempt from time-bound positive commandments; the textual root of separate ritual roles.
sources.snippet: Paraphrase: all time-bound positive commandments, men are obligated and women are exempt; all positive commandments not bound by time apply to both men and women.

sources.reference: Babylonian Talmud, Megillah 23b
sources.useFor: The minyan requirement and the Talmudic discussion of who is counted for public sanctification.
sources.snippet: Paraphrase: a quorum is required for matters of sanctity, derived from the 'congregation' of ten.

sources.reference: Judges 4:4 (Deborah)
sources.useFor: Evidence that the tradition honours female leadership/prophecy at its biblical foundation - used by reformers AND traditionalists.
sources.snippet: 'Deborah, a prophetess... she judged Israel at that time.'

sources.reference: Exodus 15:20 (Miriam) and Deuteronomy 24:1 (the get)
sources.useFor: Miriam 'the prophetess' grounds female religious stature; Deut 24:1 is the divorce verse underlying the agunah problem.
sources.snippet: 'Miriam the prophetess... took a timbrel'; Deut 24:1 paraphrase: a man writes his wife a bill of divorce and places it in her hand.

sources.reference: Blu Greenberg, On Women and Judaism: A View from Tradition (1981) - and her maxim 'where there is a rabbinic will, there is a halakhic way'
sources.useFor: The Orthodox-feminist case that change is possible from WITHIN halakha.
sources.snippet: Paraphrase: tradition and feminism need not conflict; halakhic flexibility can accommodate women's fuller participation where rabbis choose to find it.

sources.reference: Judith Plaskow, Standing Again at Sinai: Judaism from a Feminist Perspective (1990)
sources.useFor: The radical feminist critique that Judaism must be re-imagined, not merely reformed at the edges; women's absence from the Sinai narrative.
sources.snippet: Paraphrase: Jewish women have stood outside the defining categories of a male-authored tradition and must be written back into Torah and theology.

sources.reference: Conservative movement CJLS responsa (1973 minyan; 1983 JTS ordination decision)
sources.useFor: Documenting change-through-halakha as the Conservative method.
sources.snippet: Paraphrase: the law committee ruled women may be counted in the prayer quorum and, subsequently, ordained, justified through halakhic argument.

C-women|Judaism.workedThesis: While Orthodox and especially Haredi Judaism have largely maintained continuity in their teaching that women occupy a separate, home-centred religious role, the Reform and Conservative movements have driven significant change - culminating in female ordination - yet the persistence of the agunah problem across all branches reveals that the most significant shifts have been in public ritual participation rather than in the core halakhic structures governing women's lives.
paragraphPlan: Paragraph 1 (C/describe): Establish the halakhic baseline - Mishnah Kiddushin 1:7 (exemption from time-bound mitzvot), the minyan (Megillah 23b), the mechitza, and the three women's mitzvot (niddah, challah, candle-lighting). Frame this as 'separate roles', and note Hirsch's 'honoured not lesser' defence and biblical figures (Deborah, Miriam) to avoid the inferiority error.

Paragraph 2 (B/analyse): Map the spectrum of change by denomination - Reform's radical egalitarianism (Breslau 1846; Jonas 1935; Priesand 1972) vs Conservative change-through-halakha (CJLS 1973 minyan; Eilberg 1985 ordination) vs Modern Orthodox cautious expansion (Yoatzot Halacha; Sara Hurwitz Maharat 2009 / Rabba 2010) vs Haredi continuity. Analyse WHY they differ: their differing views of halakha's authority.

Paragraph 3 (A/evaluate - this is the exemplar): Evaluate the SIGNIFICANCE of change vs continuity using the agunah problem as the decisive test case, showing that ritual change has outpaced structural change and that 'significance' depends on whether you measure visibility or lived impact.

Paragraph 4 (A/evaluate): Weigh the activist/contested frontier - Women of the Wall, partnership minyanim, the ordination controversy - to evaluate whether Orthodoxy is changing 'from within' (Greenberg) or holding firm, and conclude on overall significance.
C-women|Judaism.exemplarParagraph: The significance of change in Judaism's teaching on women is most rigorously tested not by counting female rabbis but by examining where the tradition has refused to move, and the agunah ('chained woman') problem exposes precisely this. Because halakha derives the law of divorce from Deuteronomy 24:1 - the husband places the bill of divorce (get) in the wife's hand - a woman whose husband refuses to grant it cannot remarry, and crucially this rule has remained intact across the branches that otherwise diverge sharply on women. This continuity is therefore far more revealing than it first appears: the Reform movement, having abandoned the get requirement for civil divorce, sidesteps rather than solves the structural inequity; the Conservative movement, true to its change-through-halakha method, engineered the Lieberman clause (1953) and used hafka'at kiddushin within the legal system; and Modern Orthodoxy, unwilling to alter the core rule, promotes the Beth Din of America halachic prenup as a workaround rather than a reform. The significance of this pattern is that the most visible changes - Regina Jonas's 1935 ordination, Sally Priesand in 1972, Amy Eilberg in 1985, women counting in the Conservative minyan from 1973 - all concern public ritual VISIBILITY, whereas the agunah's plight concerns a woman's lived autonomy, and it is here that even the progressive movements have largely declined to dismantle the underlying structure. One could counter, with Blu Greenberg, that 'where there is a rabbinic will, there is a halakhic way' and that the prenuptial agreement proves incremental change is possible; yet the very need for a workaround, rather than an amendment, demonstrates that the foundational asymmetry endures. The most significant conclusion, then, is that Judaism's teaching on women has changed dramatically in the arena of communal participation and leadership while proving remarkably resistant to change in the legal structures that most directly govern women's freedom - meaning that the headline reforms, though real, measure the tradition's adaptability less accurately than the silences do.

ladderForThis.C: DESCRIBE the traditional teaching and name the changes: state the halakhic baseline (Kiddushin 1:7 exemption, minyan, mechitza, the three women's mitzvot) and list the key changes by movement with names and dates (Regina Jonas 1935, Sally Priesand 1972 - Reform; Amy Eilberg 1985 - Conservative; Sara Hurwitz Maharat 2009 / Rabba 2010 - Modern Orthodox). Accurate, specific, but no argument.
ladderForThis.B: ANALYSE why the movements differ: connect each position to its underlying view of halakhic authority (Reform overrides halakha on ethical grounds; Conservative changes through the CJLS within halakha; Orthodoxy holds the core law and expands only at the margins) and show how the SAME texts (e.g. Deborah, Miriam) are read to support opposite conclusions.
ladderForThis.A: EVALUATE the significance of change versus continuity: argue a defensible thesis (e.g. that ritual visibility has changed far more than the structural law, as the agunah problem proves), weigh counter-evidence (Greenberg's 'halakhic way', the halachic prenup), and judge what the pattern reveals about the tradition's real capacity to change.

trapsForThisQuestion: Calling Reform, Conservative and Orthodox 'sects' or implying one is 'more Jewish' - they are movements/denominations; describe them neutrally.
Claiming 'all Orthodox Jews' do the same thing - Modern Orthodox and Haredi differ markedly (Yoatzot Halacha and partnership minyanim vs strict separation). Specify which.

Stating women are 'inferior' in traditional Judaism - the halakhic position is 'exempt/separate roles', and traditionalists (Hirsch) explicitly frame it as honoured, not lesser. Get the framing right.
Saying Regina Jonas was American or the first US woman rabbi - she was ordained in Berlin in 1935; Sally Priesand (1972) was the first AMERICAN woman rabbi. Don't conflate them.
Treating the agunah problem as 'solved' - it is mitigated by prenups and (in Conservative/Reform) by other mechanisms, but the core Orthodox halakha is unchanged; calling it solved is an error.
Using Christian vocabulary (chapel, congregation as 'church', 'priest', 'sermon') for Jewish practice - use synagogue/shul, rabbi, minyan, Torah reading, mechitza.
Confusing the titles 'Maharat'/'Rabba' (Modern Orthodox women clergy) with full mainstream Orthodox ordination - they are contested and rejected by the RCA and OU; don't overstate consensus.
Vague filler like 'attitudes have changed over time' with no named movement, figure, date or text - every claim must be anchored to a specific.

C-science|Christianity.topic: Christianity's response to the scientific challenge of Darwinian evolution: change and continuity across denominations

whatToKnow.point: The challenge is specific: Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection, which directly contests a literal reading of Genesis 1–2 (special creation, fixed 'kinds', and humanity's unique creation in God's image).
whatToKnow.detail: Charles Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859 and 'The Descent of Man' in 1871. The threat to Christianity is threefold: (1) common descent contradicts separate creation of 'kinds' (Genesis 1:24–25); (2) natural selection offers a purposeless mechanism, displacing design; (3) human evolution from earlier primates challenges Genesis 2:7 ('the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground') and the doctrine of humans made in the imago Dei (Genesis 1:27). The age-of-the-Earth dispute (Ussher's 4004 BC chronology vs deep geological time) is a related front.

whatToKnow.point: There is NO single 'Christian' response - denominations diverge sharply, so any A-grade answer must name and contrast specific positions, not generalise.
whatToKnow.detail: The spectrum runs: Young Earth Creationism (Henry Morris, 'The Genesis Flood' 1961; Ken Ham / Answers in Genesis) rejecting evolution entirely; Intelligent Design (Michael Behe, 'Darwin's Black Box' 1996; Discovery Institute) accepting an old Earth but rejecting unguided natural selection; theistic evolution / evolutionary creationism (Francis Collins, BioLogos, 2007) fully accepting evolution as God's method; and the Catholic magisterial position. These are NOT denominations but cross-cutting interpretive stances.

whatToKnow.point: The Roman Catholic Church is the clearest example of CHANGE/accommodation over time, moving from caution to formal acceptance.
whatToKnow.detail: Pope Pius XII's encyclical 'Humani Generis' (1950) permitted evolution as a hypothesis for the human body but insisted the soul is directly created by God and defended monogenism (descent from a single Adam) to preserve original sin. Pope John Paul II told the Pontifical Academy of Sciences in 1996 that evolution is 'more than a hypothesis'. Pope Francis (2014) affirmed evolution and the Big Bang are compatible with belief in a Creator, saying God 'is not a magician with a magic wand'. Catholicism never read Genesis as a science textbook (Augustine's 'The Literal Meaning of Genesis' allowed allegory).

whatToKnow.point: American fundamentalist Protestantism is the clearest example of LACK OF CHANGE / hardened resistance, crystallised in the Scopes Trial.
whatToKnow.detail: The Scopes 'Monkey' Trial (Dayton, Tennessee, 1925) saw teacher John Scopes prosecuted under the Butler Act for teaching evolution; William Jennings Bryan prosecuted, Clarence Darrow defended. Fundamentalism (named from 'The Fundamentals', 1910–1915) treated biblical inerrancy as non-negotiable. This hardened further with Henry Morris and John Whitcomb's 'The Genesis Flood' (1961), founding modern 'creation science', and Ken Ham's Creation Museum (2007) and Ark Encounter (2016).

whatToKnow.point: Mainline and evangelical-scientist voices show genuine theological reconciliation, not just tolerance.
whatToKnow.detail: Francis Collins (director of the Human Genome Project, an evangelical) founded BioLogos (2007) and wrote 'The Language of God' (2006), arguing DNA evidence for common descent is overwhelming and God could create through evolution. Earlier, reactions among Anglican clergymen-scientists at the 1860 Oxford meeting of the British Association for the Advancement of Science were divided - but botanist Asa Gray (a devout Congregationalist, a deacon at the First Church in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and Darwin's American champion) argued evolution and design were compatible from the outset. The Anglican Communion has no doctrinal bar to evolution.

whatToKnow.point: The interpretive key dividing the camps is hermeneutics - HOW Genesis is read - not science itself.
whatToKnow.detail: Literalist/concordist readings (YEC) treat Genesis 1 as historical-scientific narrative with six 24-hour days. Non-literal readings include the 'framework' interpretation, 'day-age' theory (yom = epoch), and Augustine's much older allegorical/figurative approach. The Galileo affair (1633) is the cautionary precedent both sides cite: the Church's later admission (John Paul II, 1992) that it erred against Galileo is used to argue against repeating a literalist mistake with Darwin.

pointsOfView.label: Young Earth Creationism (rejection / no change)
pointsOfView.who: Henry Morris & John Whitcomb ('The Genesis Flood', 1961); Ken Ham / Answers in Genesis
pointsOfView.summary: Genesis 1–11 is literal history: a c.6,000–10,000-year-old Earth, six 24-hour creation days, fixed created 'kinds', and a global Flood explaining the fossil record. Evolution is incompatible with Scripture and is rejected outright; 'creation science' is offered as an alternative. Biblical inerrancy is the non-negotiable starting point.

pointsOfView.label: Intelligent Design (partial accommodation)
pointsOfView.who: Michael Behe ('Darwin's Black Box', 1996); the Discovery Institute
pointsOfView.summary: Accepts an old Earth and some common descent but argues that 'irreducibly complex' biochemical systems (e.g. the bacterial flagellum) cannot arise by unguided natural selection and point to a designer. Critics (and the 2005 Kitzmiller v. Dover ruling) judged ID to be religiously motivated and not science.

pointsOfView.label: Catholic theistic evolution (formal accommodation / change)
pointsOfView.who: Pius XII (Humani Generis 1950), John Paul II (1996), Pope Francis (2014); Pontifical Academy of Sciences
pointsOfView.summary: Evolution of the body is accepted as compatible with faith provided God directly creates each human soul. John Paul II called evolution 'more than a hypothesis'. Genesis is read theologically (the WHO and WHY of creation), not scientifically - continuous with Augustine and Aquinas, so no doctrinal crisis.

pointsOfView.label: Evangelical evolutionary creationism (reconciliation)
pointsOfView.who: Francis Collins / BioLogos (2007); Asa Gray (19th c.)
pointsOfView.summary: Fully embraces evolutionary science, including human common descent from genetic evidence, while holding orthodox Christian faith: God is the author of the evolutionary process. Genesis 1–2 conveys theological truth about purpose and relationship, not biological mechanism.

pointsOfView.label: Liberal/mainline accommodation
pointsOfView.who: Anglican and mainline Protestant leadership; Frederick Temple (later Archbishop of Canterbury)
pointsOfView.summary: Saw little conflict; many clergy-scientists in the 1860s argued evolution revealed the grandeur of God's method. No doctrinal bar to evolution exists in the Anglican Communion. The 1860 Oxford debate (Wilberforce vs Huxley) is often mythologised as 'science beats religion' but was more contested and less decisive than legend claims.

sources.reference: Genesis 1:27 (KJV): 'So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them.'
sources.useFor: Establishing the imago Dei doctrine that human evolution from primates is alleged to threaten; the core text every position must interpret.
sources.snippet: 'in the image of God created he him'

sources.reference: Genesis 2:7 (KJV): 'And the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life.'
sources.useFor: The special-creation-of-humanity text central to the Catholic 'soul is directly created' qualification and to YEC literalism.
sources.snippet: 'formed man of the dust of the ground'

sources.reference: Pius XII, 'Humani Generis' (1950), §36–37
sources.useFor: Documenting the Catholic conditional acceptance: body may evolve, soul directly created, monogenism defended for original sin.
sources.snippet: Permits research into evolution of the human body 'from pre-existing and living matter' while insisting souls are created immediately by God.

sources.reference: Charles Darwin, 'On the Origin of Species' (1859) and 'The Descent of Man' (1871)
sources.useFor: Naming the precise scientific challenge and dating it; common descent and natural selection.
sources.snippet: Species change over time via natural selection acting on heritable variation; humans share ancestry with other animals.

sources.reference: Francis Collins, 'The Language of God' (2006); BioLogos founding (2007)
sources.useFor: Evidence for evolutionary creationism: an evangelical genome scientist reconciling DNA evidence with orthodox faith.
sources.snippet: Genetic evidence for common descent is compelling; God can create through the evolutionary process.

sources.reference: Henry Morris & John Whitcomb, 'The Genesis Flood' (1961)
sources.useFor: The founding text of modern Young Earth 'creation science' and its flood geology.
sources.snippet: A global Flood, not deep time, explains the fossil-bearing strata; the Earth is young.

sources.reference: Kitzmiller v. Dover Area School District (2005); Scopes Trial (1925)
sources.useFor: Legal-historical flashpoints showing the persistence (lack of change) of literalist resistance in the USA.
sources.snippet: Dover ruled Intelligent Design was religion not science; Scopes prosecuted the teaching of evolution under the Butler Act.

C-science|Christianity.workedThesis: While popular accounts frame Christianity as monolithically hostile to Darwinian evolution, a closer analysis reveals a spectrum of responses whose significance lies in their hermeneutics: the Catholic Church and evangelical scientists like Francis Collins underwent profound accommodation because they never staked faith on a literal Genesis, whereas American fundamentalism's enduring resistance - from the 1925 Scopes Trial to Ken Ham - is significant precisely because it fused biblical inerrancy with scientific claims, making change theologically impossible.
paragraphPlan: Thesis paragraph: reject the 'religion vs science' myth; argue the variable that explains change vs continuity is HOW each group reads Genesis, and signpost the three positions to be evaluated.

Body 1 - LACK OF CHANGE: American fundamentalism. Trace inerrancy from 'The Fundamentals' (1910–15) → Scopes Trial (1925) → 'The Genesis Flood' (1961) → Ken Ham. Evaluate WHY change is impossible here: science and Scripture are made a single truth-claim, so conceding evolution = conceding biblical authority.

Body 2 - CHANGE/accommodation: Roman Catholicism. Trace Augustine's non-literalism → Humani Generis (1950, conditional) → John Paul II (1996, 'more than a hypothesis') → Francis (2014). Evaluate the significance of the soul/monogenism qualification: accommodation was possible BECAUSE doctrine was protected at the one point evolution threatened it.

Body 3 - RECONCILIATION as the most significant development: Francis Collins / BioLogos and Asa Gray. Evaluate that the deepest challenge (human common descent via DNA) produced not retreat but a positive theology of evolution-as-method - showing the conflict was never inevitable.

Evaluation/judgement paragraph: weigh which response is most historically significant. Argue the Galileo precedent (admitted error, 1992) makes the Catholic/Collins path the more sustainable, while fundamentalism's continuity is significant as a sociological/identity phenomenon more than a theological one.

Conclusion: the significance of Christianity's response is that it was hermeneutical, not scientific - the tradition that already read Genesis figuratively absorbed Darwin; the tradition that read it as science could not.
C-science|Christianity.exemplarParagraph: The most revealing case of continuity - a deliberate refusal to change - is American fundamentalist Protestantism, and its significance lies in WHY change was structurally impossible rather than merely unpopular. Fundamentalism took its name and doctrine from 'The Fundamentals' (1910–1915), which enshrined biblical inerrancy as the foundation of faith; consequently, when natural selection threatened a literal reading of Genesis 1, the threat was not to one peripheral doctrine but to the authority of Scripture itself. This is why the 1925 Scopes Trial, in which William Jennings Bryan prosecuted a Tennessee teacher under the Butler Act, was experienced as a battle for the Bible rather than a debate about biology. The position then hardened rather than softened: Morris and Whitcomb's 'The Genesis Flood' (1961) rebuilt geology around a literal global Flood and a young Earth, and Ken Ham's Creation Museum (2007) and Ark Encounter (2016) institutionalised it. Evaluating its significance, this continuity matters less as a scientific stance - mainstream biology rejects it, and the 2005 Kitzmiller v. Dover ruling barred its Intelligent Design cousin from public-school science - than as a marker of religious identity: by fusing the truth of Scripture to a scientific claim about the Earth's age, fundamentalism made any accommodation to Darwin equivalent to apostasy. The lack of change is therefore not intellectual obstinacy but the logical outworking of a hermeneutic, which is precisely what distinguishes it from the Catholic and evangelical-scientist traditions that could absorb evolution because they had never made that fusion.

ladderForThis.C: DESCRIBE/EXPLAIN: Correctly state Darwin's challenge (Origin of Species 1859; natural selection; human descent) and describe at least two distinct Christian responses with accurate names and dates - e.g. Young Earth Creationism rejects evolution and reads Genesis literally; the Catholic Church (Humani Generis 1950, John Paul II 1996) accepts evolution of the body. Accurate, specific, but largely report-style.
ladderForThis.B: ANALYSE: Explain WHY the responses differ - connect each position to its underlying hermeneutic (literal/inerrantist vs figurative/theological reading of Genesis) and to the specific doctrine at stake (imago Dei, original sin, biblical authority). Compare and contrast the camps rather than listing them, e.g. linking Catholic accommodation to its Augustinian non-literal tradition.
ladderForThis.A: EVALUATE SIGNIFICANCE / CRITICALLY ANALYSE: Make and defend a judgement about which response is most significant and why - e.g. arguing that the change-vs-continuity divide is explained by hermeneutics not science, using the Galileo precedent (1992 admission) to assess sustainability, weighing fundamentalism's continuity as identity-marker against Collins's reconciliation as theological resolution, and acknowledging the 'conflict thesis' (Wilberforce vs Huxley myth) as historically overstated.

trapsForThisQuestion: Treating 'the Christian response' as one thing - examiners penalise this heavily. You MUST name specific variants (YEC, ID, Catholic, evangelical-scientist) and contrast them.

Calling Young Earth Creationism the 'traditional' or 'original' Christian view. It is a 20th-century movement ('The Genesis Flood', 1961); Augustine (5th c.) and Aquinas read Genesis non-literally long before Darwin. Do not project modern literalism backwards.

Saying 'the Catholic Church fully accepts evolution' without the crucial qualification: it insists God directly creates each human soul (Humani Generis, 1950) - the body may evolve, the soul does not. Omitting this is an accuracy error.

Repeating the myth that the 1860 Oxford debate (Wilberforce vs Huxley) was a decisive humiliation of religion by science. Historians regard the 'conflict thesis' as overstated; a sophisticated answer notes this.
Confusing Intelligent Design with Young Earth Creationism. ID accepts an old Earth and some common descent; it argues for a designer via 'irreducible complexity' (Behe). They are distinct positions.

Conflating evolution with the Big Bang / age-of-Earth as one issue. Keep the biological challenge (natural selection, common descent) distinct from cosmology and geology, though you may note the age-of-Earth link.
Only EXPLAINING positions (C-grade behaviour) when the question asks you to evaluate SIGNIFICANCE. You must reach a defended judgement, not just catalogue views.
Vague filler like 'some Christians agree and some disagree'. Always anchor to a named figure, text and date (Collins/BioLogos 2007; Ham/AiG; Pius XII 1950).

C-science|Judaism.topic: Judaism's response to the scientific challenge of Darwinian evolution (and the age of the universe / Big Bang cosmology) - continuity and change across Orthodox, Conservative (Masorti) and Reform/Progressive movements

whatToKnow.point: Judaism has no single 'official' position on evolution; responses fracture along the movement lines that formed in the 19th century - the same century evolution appeared.
whatToKnow.detail: Reform Judaism began with the 1885 Pittsburgh Platform (Kaufmann Kohler), which explicitly accepted that science and modern thought are compatible with Judaism. Conservative/Masorti Judaism (Solomon Schechter, Jewish Theological Seminary) and Modern Orthodoxy also accommodated science, while Haredi (ultra-Orthodox) Judaism largely resisted. Charles Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859, the same generation these movements were crystallising, so Judaism's response is inseparable from its internal denominational split.

whatToKnow.point: A long pre-Darwinian Jewish tradition of NON-LITERAL Genesis reading meant many Jews did not experience evolution as a crisis the way biblical literalists did.
whatToKnow.detail: Maimonides (Rambam, 1138-1204) in the 'Guide for the Perplexed' (Moreh Nevuchim) argued that where reason/science conclusively proves something, apparently contradictory scripture must be read allegorically - he said had Aristotle proven the universe eternal, he would have reinterpreted Genesis. Nachmanides (Ramban, 1194-1270) read the 'days' of creation as vast eras. This interpretive flexibility is the KEY reason Judaism's response shows more continuity (absorbing science) than rupture.

whatToKnow.point: Rav Abraham Isaac Kook gave evolution a positive theological reading from WITHIN Orthodoxy - change framed as continuity.
whatToKnow.detail: Rav Avraham Yitzchak HaCohen Kook (1865-1935), first Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of British Mandate Palestine, welcomed evolution in his writings: he saw the theory of the gradual ascent of species as harmonising with Kabbalistic and mystical ideas of the world's spiritual evolution toward perfection. He treated evolution as no threat because Torah concerns meaning/ethics, not biology textbooks. (Note: his collection 'Orot HaKodesh' was compiled and published posthumously, 1963-64, so this is not a tidy single 1900s 'response to Darwin' treatise.) This is the strongest example of an Orthodox figure ASSIMILATING rather than rejecting science.

whatToKnow.point: The Haredi rejection / 'lack of change' pole is real and was dramatised by the 2004-2005 Slifkin affair.
whatToKnow.detail: Rabbi Natan Slifkin ('the Zoo Rabbi') wrote books reconciling Torah with evolution and an old universe. In 2004-05 approximately twenty-three leading Haredi rabbis (gedolim) banned his books as heresy (kefirah), insisting on a young universe (~5,786 years per the Hebrew calendar) and rejecting common-descent evolution. This shows the genuine 'lack of change' strand and that the debate is LIVE, not historical.

whatToKnow.point: Reform and Conservative Judaism actively EMBRACE evolution; some use it constructively in theology.
whatToKnow.detail: Reform Judaism treats the Torah as humanly authored and divinely inspired, so evolutionary biology poses no doctrinal problem. Mordecai Kaplan (1881-1983), founder of Reconstructionist Judaism, reframed God as a naturalistic 'power that makes for salvation', fully at home with science. Many Conservative thinkers read Genesis 1 as theological poetry about order and purpose, not a scientific timeline.

whatToKnow.point: The cosmology angle (age of universe / Big Bang) parallels evolution and adds a striking convergence claim.
whatToKnow.detail: The Hebrew calendar dates creation to roughly 5,786 years ago, clashing with a 13.8-billion-year-old universe. Nachmanides' era-reading and Gerald Schroeder (MIT-trained physicist, 'Genesis and the Big Bang', 1990) argue the six days and billions of years can be reconciled via relativistic time-dilation. This is a contested individual apologetic, not accepted mainstream physics and not an official Orthodox position - Schroeder is one individual, not a movement. The Big Bang itself was sometimes welcomed by religious thinkers as echoing 'a beginning' (Genesis 1:1).

pointsOfView.label: Orthodox embrace (theological synthesis)
pointsOfView.who: Rav Abraham Isaac Kook (1865-1935), Religious Zionist Orthodoxy
pointsOfView.summary: Evolution is no threat and can even be welcomed: it mirrors the mystical/Kabbalistic vision of a cosmos gradually ascending toward spiritual perfection. Torah teaches meaning and ethics, not biology, so science and Torah operate on different planes and need not conflict.

pointsOfView.label: Rationalist precedent (non-literal Genesis)
pointsOfView.who: Maimonides (Rambam, 1138-1204) and Nachmanides (Ramban, 1194-1270), medieval interpretation
pointsOfView.summary: Where reason or demonstrated science conflicts with the plain text, scripture should be read allegorically; the 'days' of creation can mean vast eras. This medieval flexibility is why many Jews never treated evolution as a faith crisis.
pointsOfView.source: Guide for the Perplexed (Maimonides); Ramban's Torah commentary on Genesis 1

pointsOfView.label: Haredi rejection (continuity / lack of change)
pointsOfView.who: The approximately twenty-three gedolim who banned Slifkin's books, 2004-05; mainstream Haredi Judaism
pointsOfView.summary: A literal young universe (~5,786 years) and special creation are non-negotiable; common-descent evolution is heresy (kefirah). Modern science must yield to Torah and the sages (Chazal), not the reverse.
pointsOfView.source: The Slifkin ban (2004-05); traditional reading of Genesis 1 and the Hebrew calendar

pointsOfView.label: Progressive acceptance
pointsOfView.who: Reform Judaism (Pittsburgh Platform 1885, Kaufmann Kohler); Reconstructionism (Mordecai Kaplan)
pointsOfView.summary: Torah is divinely inspired human literature, not a science text, so evolution is simply accepted. Kaplan's naturalism makes science and faith fully compatible; Genesis 1 is read as theology about order, purpose and human dignity.
pointsOfView.source: Pittsburgh Platform (1885); Kaplan, Judaism as a Civilization (1934)

pointsOfView.label: Modern reconciliation (physics apologetic)
pointsOfView.who: Gerald Schroeder (b.1938), Orthodox physicist
pointsOfView.summary: Schroeder argues the six days and 13.8 billion years are both true from different relativistic frames of reference, and that Genesis read carefully anticipates Big Bang cosmology. This is a contested individual apologetic, not mainstream science and not an official Orthodox stance - useful as one reconciliation attempt, but a student must not present it as accepted physics.
pointsOfView.source: Genesis and the Big Bang (1990); The Science of God (1997)

sources.reference: Genesis 1:1 (Tanakh, JPS): 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.'
sources.useFor: The contested text itself; cosmology debate ('a beginning' echoing Big Bang) and the basis of literal vs allegorical readings.
sources.snippet: 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.'

sources.reference: Genesis 1:24 (Tanakh): 'Let the earth bring forth the living creature after its kind...'
sources.useFor: Special-creation 'after its kind' vs common descent - the exact verse the evolution debate turns on.
sources.snippet: 'Let the earth bring forth living creatures after their kinds.'
